6.55: PeriedrAmon holen ten chOran ekeinEn kai Erxanto epi tois krabattois tous kakOs echontas peripherein hopou Ekouon hoti estin — they ran throughout that whole region and began to carry around on mats those who were sick to wherever they heard he was — the response-pattern of 1:32-34 and 3:7-12 is repeated in summary form: peripherein (carrying around, transporting) the sick on krabattos mats (the low-status sleeping mat of 2:4,9,11,12) to wherever Jesus is. The running (periEdAmon = ran around) suggests urgency and energy. The whole region mobilizes to find Jesus.
6.56: Kai hopou an eisEporeueto eis kOmas E eis poleis E eis agrous, en tais agorais etithEsan tous asthenountas kai parEkaloun auton hina kan tou kraspEdou tou himatiou autou hapsOntai — and wherever he went into villages or into cities or into farms, they placed the sick in the marketplaces and were begging him that they might touch even the fringe of his garment — kraspEdon tou himatiou (fringe/edge of the garment) — in Matthew 23:5 the kraspEda are the tassels (tsitzit) commanded in Num 15:38-40 as reminders of the commandments. The fringe-touch echoes 5:27-28 (the hemorrhaging woman's garment-touch). The summary of healing concludes the Galilee section: hosos an hEpsanto autou esOzonto — as many as touched him were healed/saved.

7.34: ephphatha — Ephphatha — another Aramaic preservation (cf. 5:41); the word itself becomes performative: Jesus says be opened and the ears open. Mogilalos (v.32, speaking with difficulty or mute) is the LXX word in Isa 35:6 (the tongue of the mute will sing for joy) — Mark uses the Isaiah-term for the man healed, signaling that the Isaianic healing-sign is being enacted. The detail of Jesus's sighing (estenaxen) before the healing is a characteristic Markan note of Jesus's genuine human emotion and the weight he carries in the healing-mission.
7.1: hoi Pharisaioi kai tines tōn grammateōn elthontes apo Hierosolymōn — Pharisees and some of the scribes having come from Jerusalem — the delegation from the capital marks an escalation: this is not local scribal scrutiny (as in 2:6; 3:22) but an official investigation mounted from Jerusalem. The combination of Pharisees (lay pietists concerned with halakhic observance) and scribes (legal scholars and Torah-interpreters) constitutes a joint inquiry into the movement's practices.
7.2: koinais chersin, tout' estin aniptois — with common hands, that is, unwashed — koinos (common, defiled, ritually impure) is the LXX and NT technical term for the state of ritual impurity; the parenthetical "that is, unwashed" (tout' estin aniptois) is Mark's aside explaining Jewish purity practice to Gentile readers unfamiliar with the custom. The accusation is halakhic non-compliance, not hygiene.
7.3: pugmē — the term is lexically debated: (1) "with a fist" = scrubbing vigorously with a cupped hand; (2) "up to the wrist/elbow" (a few MSS support a distance-measure reading). UBS5/NA28 reads pugmē. Whatever the specific gesture, the parenthetical vv.3–4 is a Markan explanatory aside for non-Jewish readers, establishing the traditional practice before Jesus's ruling on it.
7.4: kai apo agoras ean mē baptisōntai ouk esthiousin — and from the marketplace unless they immerse they do not eat — baptizō (to dip, immerse) here refers to ritual immersion after contact with potentially impure persons in the marketplace. The additional listed practices — immersing cups, pitchers, and copper vessels, and klinōn (dining couches) in some manuscripts — show how pervasively ritual purity governed daily domestic life. Defilement could pass through objects, not only persons.
7.5: dia ti ou peripatousin hoi mathētai sou kata tēn paradosin tōn presbyterōn — why do your disciples not walk according to the tradition of the elders — paradosis (tradition, what is handed over/passed down — from paradidonai) is the disputed term. The scribes frame the issue as violation of ancestral custom rather than Torah itself. Jesus's response will reverse the frame: it is the tradition that violates Torah.
7.6: ho de eipen autois, Kalōs eprophēteusen Hsaias peri hymōn tōn hypokritōn — and he said to them: rightly Isaiah prophesied about you hypocrites — hypokritēs (actor wearing a mask, stage-performer) is the word for theatrical performance in Greek; those who speak lines not their own character. The citation follows: Isaiah 29:13 LXX. houtos ho laos tois cheilesin me tima, hē de kardia autōn porrō apechei ap' emou — this people honors me with lips but their heart is far from me — the lips/heart contrast is the prophetic anatomy of empty religion: external speech without inward reality.
7.7: matēn de sebontai me, didaskontes didaskalias entalmata anthrōpōn — they worship me in vain, teaching as doctrines human commandments — matēn (in vain, without purpose, to no effect — the worship achieves nothing). The LXX version of Isaiah 29:13 is actually stronger at this point than the Hebrew MT, using entalmata anthrōpōn (human commandments/injunctions) to characterize the teaching. The human origin of the tradition (anthrōpōn) is contrasted throughout with the divine origin of the law (entolē tou theou in vv.8–9).
7.8: aphentes tēn entolēn tou theou krateite tēn paradosin tōn anthrōpōn — letting go of the commandment of God you hold the tradition of men — entolē (commandment, from entellomai = to give authoritative command) vs. paradosis (tradition, secondary transmission). The opposition is structural: divine commandment released, human tradition grasped. The priority is inverted: what God directly commanded is treated as secondary to what the elders subsequently taught.
7.9: kalōs atheteite tēn entolēn tou theou hina tēn paradosin hymōn stēsēte — rightly you set aside the commandment of God in order to establish your tradition — kalōs is bitterly ironic: "nicely/skillfully you do it." atheteō (to set aside, declare void — from a- + tithēmi = to un-place, unmake the position of) is the legal term for rendering a ruling or obligation invalid. The purpose clause hina ... stēsēte (in order that you might establish) exposes the implicit logic: tradition is upheld at Torah's expense.
7.10: Mōysēs gar eipen, Tima ton patera sou kai tēn mētera sou — for Moses said: Honor your father and your mother — the Moses-said formula grounds the commandment in Sinai authority (Exod 20:12; Deut 5:16). The second citation — "the one who curses father or mother, let him die" (Exod 21:17; Lev 20:9) — is the capital-penalty provision that underlines how seriously Torah treats parental honor. Jesus establishes the Torah's own severity before exposing the tradition's circumvention of it.
7.11: korban, ho estin dōron — Korban, which is a gift — korban is the Aramaic loanword for a gift dedicated to the temple treasury (Hebrew qorban, from qarab = to draw near, to offer). Once resources were declared korban — dedicated to God — they could not legally be redirected, even to support parents in material need. The tradition thus created a halakhically valid mechanism by which a son could exempt himself from financial parental support (ou mē ōphelēthēs ex emou = you will not be benefited from me) by invoking a temple-dedication vow.
7.12: ouketi aphiete auton ouden poiēsai tō patri ē tē mētri — you no longer permit him to do anything for his father or mother — the legal consequence: the korban-declaration extinguishes all subsequent obligation to parents. The tradition has not merely supplemented Torah; it has generated a loophole that makes Torah compliance impossible once the vow is made. Jesus's case study concretizes the abstract charge of v.8–9 with a specific mechanism.
7.13: akyrountes ton logon tou theou tē paradosei hymōn hē paredōkate — nullifying the word of God by your tradition which you have handed on — akyrouō (to make without authority, to annul — from a- + kyrios = making the Lord's ruling invalid) is the antonym of kyroō (to confirm, validate). The tradition (paradosis) has accumulated institutional authority sufficient to effectively override Torah. "And many such things like this you do" — the korban case is one instance of a systemic pattern, not an isolated exception.
7.14: kai proskalesamenos palin ton ochlon elegen autois — and calling the crowd to himself again he said — Jesus widens the audience from the Pharisees/scribes (vv.1–13) to the crowd. The teaching that follows is addressed publicly. Akouete mou pantes kai suniete — hear me all and understand — the double imperative with pantes (all) marks this as a universal public declaration, not a private rulings addressed only to the questioning party.
7.15: ouden estin exōthen tou anthrōpou eisporeuomenon eis auton ho dynatai koinōsai auton — nothing outside the person entering into him is able to defile him — koinoō (to make common, to render ritually impure) is the verb throughout the pericope. The principle reverses the Levitical purity paradigm: defilement moves outward from the person, not inward from outside. The implication for the food-law controversy is radical: what is eaten (external to the person) cannot be the source of defilement.
7.16: Many manuscripts include ei tis echei ōta akouein akouetō — if anyone has ears to hear, let him hear — as v.16, an interpolation from 4:9,23. UBS5/NA28 brackets or omits it. If original, it would signal this teaching as parabolic in character, requiring inner-ear understanding rather than surface-level hearing — consistent with the private explanation that follows in vv.17–23.
7.17: kai hote eisēlthen eis oikon apo tou ochlou, epērōtōn auton hoi mathētai autou tēn parabolēn — and when he entered a house away from the crowd his disciples asked him about the parable — the house/crowd spatial division recurs (cf. 4:10; 7:24; 9:28; 10:10): private explanation follows public proclamation. parabolē is used broadly for any dark or enigmatic saying, not only narrative parables. Outōs kai hymeis asyntetoi este — are you also without understanding — asynetos (without comprehension — from a- + syniēmi = failing to put it together) is the word for those who cannot assemble the evidence into understanding; cf. 8:17, 21.
7.18: ou noeite hoti pan to exōthen eisporeuomenon eis ton anthrōpon ou dynatai auton koinōsai — do you not understand that nothing entering the person from outside is able to defile him — noeō (to perceive with the mind, to grasp intellectually — from nous) is paired with the negative ou: the disciples' cognitive failure is named. The re-statement of v.15 is slightly expanded with the digestive explanation that follows in v.19: what enters goes into the stomach, not the heart (kardia), and is expelled — the physical process cannot touch the moral centre.
7.19: hoti ouk eisporeuetai autou eis tēn kardian all' eis tēn koilian, kai eis ton aphedrōna ekporeuetai, katharizōn panta ta brōmata — because it does not enter his heart but his stomach and goes out into the latrine, cleansing all foods — the parenthetical katharizōn panta ta brōmata (cleansing/declaring clean all foods) is Mark's editorial aside identifying the ruling implication of Jesus's teaching. This phrase is absent from Matthew's parallel (15:17). It is the most significant halakhic ruling in the Gospel traditions, effectively overturning the Levitical dietary laws (Lev 11), and anticipates the vision in Acts 10:15 and Paul's teaching in Romans 14. The subject of katharizōn is disputed: the grammatically nearest antecedent is Jesus, making this a Markan declaration that Jesus declared all foods clean.
7.20: to de ek tou anthrōpou ekporeuomenon, ekeino koinoi ton anthrōpon — but what comes out of the person, that defiles the person — the inverted principle is stated explicitly: defilement has a single source, the human interior. The list that follows (vv.21–22) catalogs what the interior produces. The contrast with Levitical purity (which assigned defilement to external objects, foods, bodily discharges) is complete: for Jesus, the heart is the source, not the skin or stomach.
7.21: esōthen gar ek tēs kardias tōn anthrōpōn hoi dialogismoi hoi kakoi ekporeuontai — for from within, from the heart of persons, the evil reasonings come out — dialogismos (reasoning, deliberation — from dialogizomai = to reason through; in NT consistently the internal deliberation that produces moral conclusions) is the first item: cognitive evil, the mind's disposition. The following list of 6 plural nouns (fornications, thefts, murders, adulteries, covetings, wickednesses) and 6 singular nouns (deceit, sensuality, evil eye, slander, arrogance, folly) is a catalog of inward moral corruption, not ritual violation.
7.22: aselgeia — sensuality, licentiousness (the absence of moral restraint); ophthalmos ponēros — evil eye — in Semitic idiom, the evil eye is envy and miserliness (Matt 6:23; Prov 23:6 LXX), not an occult gaze; blasphēmia — slander, defamation (of persons as well as God); hyperēphania — arrogance, haughtiness (from hyper + phainomai = appearing above others — the pride of social superiority); aphrosynē — folly, senselessness (the moral failure of not acknowledging God). The list ends with the foolishness that Proverbs names as the foundation of all moral failure.
7.23: panta tauta ta ponēra esōthen ekporeuetai kai koinoi ton anthrōpon — all these evil things come out from within and defile the person — the concluding summary applies koinoō (defile) to the list. The pericope's argument is complete: the tradition's application of koinōsis to unwashed hands is misapplied; the real defilement comes from the moral interior, not from physical contact with food or persons.
7.24: kai ekeithen anastas apēlthen eis ta horia Tyrou — and rising from there he departed into the regions of Tyre — Tyre is on the Phoenician coast approximately 35 miles northwest of Capernaum — Gentile territory entirely outside Jewish Galilee. kai eiselthōn eis oikian oudena ēthelen gnōnai, kai ouk ēdynasthē lathein — and entering a house he wanted no one to know, and he was unable to escape notice — the Messianic Secret maintained: private residence, no-announcement intention. But his reputation precedes him even into Gentile regions; the attempt at concealment fails immediately.
7.25: all' eutheōs akousasa gynē peri autou, hēs eichen to thygatrion autēs pneuma akatharton — but immediately a woman whose little daughter had an unclean spirit, having heard about him — eutheōs again; the woman's approach is immediate upon hearing. thygatrion (little daughter — diminutive of thygatēr) is the affectionate parental form, as with Jairus's daughter (5:23). prosepesen pros tous podas autou (fell at his feet) — the posture of extreme supplication, as with Jairus (5:22) and the Gerasene man (5:33). The same gesture crosses cultural boundaries.
7.26: hē de gynē ēn Hellēnis, Syrophoinikissa tō genei — the woman was Greek/Gentile, Syrophoenician by race — Hellēnis (Hellenist — Greek-speaking, Gentile by culture) identifies her cultural orientation; Syrophoinikissa tō genei (Syrophoenician by race/descent) specifies ethnic origin — Phoenician Syria as distinct from Libyan Phoenicia. She is doubly marked as non-Jewish, and her request is that Jesus cast the demon out of her daughter. The initial response Jesus gives (v.27) will test the mission-boundary question directly.
7.27: aphes prōton chortasthēnai ta tekna — let the children first be satisfied/filled — prōton (first) is the key word: it acknowledges mission-priority (Israel before Gentiles) without making that priority absolute or eternal. chortazō (to be filled, satisfied — used for feeding animals and, in the Beatitude, those who hunger) matches the feeding-miracle language. ou gar kalon estin labein ton arton tōn teknōn kai tois kynarois balein — for it is not good to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs — kynarion (little dog, household dog — diminutive) is a softened form; kyōn (the street-dog insult) is not used. The children-dogs metaphor maps covenant Israel (tekna) vs. Gentiles (kynaria) with the ordering acknowledged but not the exclusion made permanent.
7.28: kyrie, kai ta kynarion hypokato tēs trapezēs esthiousin apo tōn psichōn tōn paidōn — Lord, even the little dogs under the table eat from the children's crumbs — kyrie (Lord/Master) is her address, acknowledging his authority. The woman accepts the image and works within its logic: she does not claim the children's portion but only the psichōn (crumbs, fragments — the scraps that fall from the table). The reply is neither an argument against the ordering nor a rejection of it; it works within the metaphor to find space for Gentile participation at its margins. The theological brilliance is that she claims the overflow of the children's blessing, not the blessing itself.
7.29: dia touton ton logon hypage — because of this word/saying, go — the woman's logos (word, response, saying) is what Jesus names as the reason for the healing. In Matthew's parallel (15:28) it is her faith (pistis) that is named; in Mark it is the word she spoke. The healing is pronounced before any physical contact or return home: Jesus declares the demon departed (exelēlythen to daimonion ek tēs thygatros sou = the demon has gone out from your daughter) at a distance and in the past tense. The departure has already occurred.
7.30: kai apelthousa eis ton oikon autēs heuren to paidion beblēmenon epi tēn klinēn kai to daimonion exelēlythos — going to her house she found the child lying on the bed and the demon gone — both participles are perfects: beblēmenon (having been cast/laid — perfect passive of ballō) and exelēlythos (having gone out — perfect active of exerchomai). The perfect tenses indicate completed states at the time of discovery — the healing had already occurred, precisely when Jesus's word was spoken. The physical verification confirms the remotely-spoken pronouncement.
7.31: kai palin exelthōn ek tōn horiōn Tyrou ēlthen dia Sidōnos eis tēn thalassan tēs Galilaias ana meson tōn horiōn Dekapoleōs — and leaving the region of Tyre he came through Sidon to the Sea of Galilee through the middle of the region of the Decapolis — the route is geographically unusual: Sidon is north of Tyre, making the path north-then-south before arriving at Galilee via the eastern Decapolis. Some scholars have proposed a geographical error; others read it as an extended Gentile mission circuit. The Decapolis (ana meson = through the middle) is where the Gerasene had been sent as missionary (5:20): Jesus moves through the territory that man had evangelized.
7.32: kai pherosin autō kōphon kai mogillalon kai parakalosin auton hina epithē autō tēn cheira — and they brought to him a deaf man speaking with difficulty and they begged him to lay his hand on him — kōphos (deaf/mute — Greek uses one word for both) describes the primary impairment; mogilalos (speaking with difficulty, barely able to speak — from mogis = scarcely + laleō = speak) is a rare word that appears in the Greek OT (LXX) at Isaiah 35:6: "the tongue of the mute (mogilalōn) will shout for joy." Mark's use of the Isaiah-vocabulary signals that the messianic healing-sign of the Isaianic new exodus is being enacted.
7.33: kai apolabomenos auton apo tou ochlou kat' idian — taking him apart from the crowd privately — the withdrawal from public space for private healing recurs (5:40; 8:23): the healing is not a public performance. The physical method: (1) ebalen tous daktylous autou eis ta ōta autou (put his fingers into the man's ears); (2) ptysas hēpsato tēs glōssēs autou (having spat he touched his tongue) — spittle used as a healing medium, consistent with 8:23. The physical contact with the affected organs is characteristic of Mark's healing accounts; it is not magic but the embodied encounter of divine healing-power with human impairment.
7.35: kai eutheōs diēnoichthēsan autou hai akoai, kai elythē ho desmos tēs glōssēs autou, kai elalei orthōs — and immediately his hearing was opened, and the bond of his tongue was loosed, and he spoke plainly — eutheōs again; the command was immediately effective. diēnoichthēsan (were opened — same verb-root as ephphatha). desmos (bond, restraint — what bound the tongue is now loosed) is released: elythē (was loosed, untied — aorist passive of lyō). orthōs (rightly, plainly, correctly — the adverb of straight/right) describes the quality of restored speech: not simply audible but properly formed.
7.36: kai diestellato autois hina mēdeni legōsin — and he commanded them to tell no one — the Messianic Secret command (cf. 1:44; 5:43; 7:36); this is the seventh silence-command in Mark. Hoson de autois diestelleto, autoi mallon perissoteron ekērysson — but the more he commanded them the more abundantly they proclaimed it — the paradox intensifies: the command is categorical, the crowd's response is the inverse. perissoteron (more abundantly, excessively) — the more he prohibited, the more emphatically they proclaimed. The Messianic Secret cannot be contained by human command.
7.37: kalōs panta pepoiēken, kai tous kōphous poiei akouein kai tous alalous lalein — he has done all things well, and the deaf he makes hear and the mute speak — kalōs panta pepoiēken (he has done all things well — perfect tense: the completed result stands) is widely recognized as an echo of Genesis 1:31 LXX: kai eiden ho theos ta panta hosa epoiēsen, kai idou kala lian (God saw everything he had made and behold it was very good). The crowd's acclamation of the healer uses the creation-completion formula, identifying Jesus's restoration-work as the new creation. The specific healings named — deaf hearing, mute speaking — are the Isaiah 35:5–6 messianic signs, completing the intertextual web from v.32.

8.29: hymeis de tina me legete einai — but who do you say that I am? — the crucial Christological question placed at the Gospel's structural midpoint (ch.8 is the pivot between Galilee-ministry and Jerusalem-passion in Mark). su ei ho Christos — you are the Christ — Peter's confession without the fuller Matthew expansion (Son of the living God). Immediately after the confession, the Messianic Secret is commanded (v.30) and the passion prediction follows (v.31), connecting Messiahship directly to suffering and rejection. In Mark, you cannot confess the Messiah without immediately being told about the cross.
8.34: ei tis thelei opiso mou akolouthein, aparnessastho heauton kai arato ton stauron autou kai akoloutheitō moi — if anyone wishes to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me — the three imperatives are sequential: (1) aparnessastho heauton (deny himself = disown the self's claims on its own life); (2) arato ton stauron autou (take up his cross = adopt the posture of the condemned man carrying his instrument of execution); (3) akoloutheitō moi (follow me = continue in discipleship to the one walking to his death). Cross-bearing in Mark's context is not metaphor but literal: the disciples are called to the same path of suffering their master walks.
8.1: Kai palin pollou ochlou ontos kai mē echontōn ti phagōsin — and again with a great crowd and they having nothing to eat — palin (again) links the second feeding explicitly to the first (6:35–44). The setting is implied Gentile territory east of Galilee (7:31 placed Jesus in the Decapolis). The pastoral crisis — large crowd, no food — is structurally identical to the first feeding, which will make the disciples' confusion in v.4 more pointed.
8.2: Splanchnizomai epi ton ochlon — I have compassion for the crowd — splanchnizomai (to be moved in the innards — the strongest Greek emotion-word; the word for gut-level compassion) is the same verb as in 6:34 (Jesus's compassion before the first feeding). The repetition is not accidental: both feedings are driven by the same inward motivation. The duration: three days (treis hēmeras) without food. The fast is already extended beyond the point of polite hunger into physical necessity.
8.3: kai tines autōn apo makrothen hēkasin — and some of them have come from far away — makrothen (from a distance, from afar) appears in Ephesians 2:13,17 and Acts 2:39 for Gentiles who were "far off" but are drawn near in Christ. The word does not prove the crowd is Gentile but is consistent with the Decapolis location. The physical risk is genuine: they will be exhausted (eklythēsontai en tē hodō = will be undone on the road) without food on the journey home.
8.4: kai apekrithēsan autō hoi mathētai autou hoti pothen touton dynēsetai tis hōde chortasai artōn — and his disciples answered him: from where will anyone be able to satisfy these with bread in this isolated place — the disciples' question mirrors the first feeding question (6:37): the logistical impossibility is stated again despite the prior miracle. Mark presents the repetition without explanation; only in 8:17–21 will Jesus name the disciples' failure to draw the inference as spiritual hardness. erēmia (isolated place, wilderness) is the word for the desert that echoes the Exodus feeding-tradition.
8.5: hepta artous — seven loaves — versus five in the first feeding (6:38). The difference in numbers (and the different basket-counts that follow) supports reading the two feedings as distinct events rather than doublets: five and twelve are associated with Israel (five books of Moses; twelve tribes/apostles); seven and seven are associated with completeness/Gentile fullness (the Gentile nations). Mark preserves both accounts with the numerical differences intact.
8.6: kai paraggellei tō ochlō anapesein epi tēs gēs — and he instructed the crowd to recline on the ground — the seating arrangement echoes the first feeding (6:39–40: the green grass, the groups). kai labōn tous hepta artous eucharistēsas eklasen kai edidou tois mathētais autou hina paratithōsin — and having taken the seven loaves, having given thanks, he broke them and gave to the disciples to set before the crowd — eucharisteō (to give thanks — the verb that becomes the technical term for the Eucharist in Paul's institution-account: 1 Cor 11:24; Luke 22:17,19). Mark uses both eulogēō (6:41, in the first feeding) and eucharisteō (8:6) across the two accounts; the Eucharistic vocabulary is in both, not only the second.
8.7: kai eichon ichthydia oliga, kai eulogēsas auta eipen kai tauta paratithenai — and they had a few small fish, and having blessed them he said to set these before them also — the fish are handled separately with their own blessing (eulogēsas). The separation of bread and fish in the account maintains the two-element structure of both feeding accounts, consistent with the tradition being independently transmitted and not a scribal copy of the first account.
8.8: kai ephagon kai echortasthēsan kai ēran perissemata klasmatōn hepta spyridas — and they ate and were satisfied, and they picked up seven baskets of broken pieces — spyris (large flexible basket — the type used to lower Paul over the Damascus wall: Acts 9:25) is different from kophinos (6:43; the standard Jewish basket, mentioned by Juvenal as the prop of Jewish street-vendors in Rome). The basket-type change between the two accounts, combined with the location in Gentile territory, supports reading the second feeding as directed toward a Gentile crowd — though Mark does not make this explicit.
8.9: Ēsan de hōs tetrakischilioi — and they were about four thousand — the smaller number (4,000 vs. 5,000) is one of several numerical differences between the two feeding accounts that Jesus will recall precisely in vv.19–20 as evidence the disciples should have understood the significance. kai apelysen autous — and he dismissed them — the crowd is sent away; the mission moves on.
8.10: kai euthys embas eis to ploion meta tōn mathētōn autou ēlthen eis ta merē Dalmanutha — and immediately getting into the boat with his disciples he came to the region of Dalmanutha — Dalmanutha is otherwise unattested in ancient sources; Matthew's parallel (15:39) reads Magadan/Magdala. The location is likely on the western shore of Galilee, Jewish territory, which explains the immediate Pharisaic encounter in v.11.
8.11: kai exēlthon hoi Pharisaioi kai ērxanto syzētein autō, zētountes par' autou sēmeion apo ouranou peirazōntes auton — and the Pharisees came out and began to argue with him, seeking from him a sign from heaven, testing him — syzēteō (to debate jointly, to argue against) indicates adversarial engagement, not honest inquiry. sēmeion apo ouranou (a sign from heaven) contrasts with the earthly healings already performed: they demand a cosmic confirmatory sign of a different order. peirazō (to test, to tempt) is the same word as Jesus's wilderness testing by Satan (1:13) — the Pharisees' demand is framed as Satanic-scale opposition to Jesus's mission.
8.12: kai anastenaxas tō pneumati autou legei, Ti hē genea hautē zētei sēmeion? — and sighing deeply in his spirit he says: why does this generation seek a sign? — anastenaxas (sighing deeply — intensified form of stenazō from 7:34, with ana- prefix adding depth) is located tō pneumati autou (in his spirit — the deep inner seat of the person), not merely a physical sigh. The sigh registers the profound spiritual failure the demand represents. amēn legō hymin, ei dothēsetai tē genea tautē sēmeion — the amēn oath-formula (ei + unfulfilled condition = "if a sign will be given... [it will not]") is an absolute refusal with the strongest possible assertive form. genea (generation, contemporaries) is used pejoratively throughout Mark's central section (8:38; 9:19).
8.13: kai apheis autous palin embas apēlthen eis to peran — and leaving them again getting in he departed to the other side — the abrupt departure without further engagement characterizes Mark's ending of controversy scenes with the Pharisees: they receive no debate, no sign, no engagement — only the flat refusal of v.12 and Jesus's immediate departure. The crossing to the other side removes him from their vicinity without explanation or farewell.
8.14: kai epelathonto labein artous, kai ei mē hena arton ouk eichon meth' heautōn en tō ploiō — and they forgot to take bread, except they had one loaf with them in the boat — the disciples' forgetfulness is the occasion for Jesus's teaching in vv.15–21. The hena arton (one loaf) in the boat is noted with precision; the disciples' anxiety about it in v.16 is ironic given the two feedings immediately preceding. Some interpreters read the "one loaf" as a Christological symbol — the One Bread who is with them in the boat — but Mark does not make this explicit.
8.15: horate, blepete apo tēs zymēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai tēs zymēs Hērōdou — watch out, beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and the leaven of Herod — zymē (leaven, yeast) as metaphor for pervasive invisible influence: a small amount leavens the whole batch (1 Cor 5:6; Gal 5:9). The pairing of Pharisees and Herod combines religious and political opposition: Pharisees are the religious establishment seeking a sign (v.11); Herod is the political authority who executed John (6:14–29) and who was troubled by reports about Jesus (6:16). The leaven-metaphor names their shared characteristic: a corrupting influence that spreads undetected.
8.16: kai dielogizonto pros allēlous legontes hoti artous ouk echousin — and they reasoned with one another saying: it is because we have no bread — the disciples hear "leaven" (zymē) and immediately think bread (artous) — a misinterpretation on the level of Nicodemus hearing "born again" and thinking of physical birth (John 3:4). Their reasoning (dielogizonto — from dialogizomai = to reason through, same root as dialogismos in 2:6) is entirely literal; they have reduced a spiritual warning to a logistical concern about food supply.
8.17: kai gnous legei autois, Ti dialogizesthe hoti artous ouk echete? — and knowing he says to them: why do you reason that you have no bread? — gnous (having perceived, having known — the immediate inward perception also used in 2:8 and 5:30) is Jesus's characteristic spiritual awareness of what is unspoken. oupō noeite oud' syniete? pepōrōmenēn echete tēn kardian hymōn? — do you not yet perceive or understand? is your heart hardened? — pepōrōmenēn (hardened — perfect passive participle of pōroō = to harden, callous; pōros is a callus or calcification) is the Isaiah-language for spiritual insensibility (Isa 6:10; cf. Mark 3:5 where Jesus grieves the Pharisees' hardened hearts). The disciples are diagnosed with the same condition as their opponents.
8.18: ophtalmous echontes ou blepete kai ōta echontes ouk akouete? kai ou mnēmoneuete? — having eyes you do not see, and having ears you do not hear? and do you not remember? — the seeing-without-seeing and hearing-without-hearing are the prophetic diagnosis of covenant-hardened Israel (Jer 5:21; Ezek 12:2) now applied to the Twelve. The disciples have been given the mystery of the kingdom (4:11) but their perception remains blocked. The third question (kai ou mnēmoneuete = and do you not remember?) anticipates the recall of the two feedings in vv.19–20.
8.19: hote tous pente artous eklasen eis tous pentakischilious, posous kophinous klasmatōn plēreis ērate? — when I broke the five loaves for the five thousand, how many baskets of fragments did you pick up? — the numerical recall is precise: five loaves, five thousand, twelve kophinus. The disciples answer correctly (dōdeka). The two feedings are not interchangeable events; the distinct numbers (five/twelve; seven/seven) are the evidence that should have generated theological inference about who was performing them.
8.20: Hote tous hepta eis tous tetrakischilious, posōn spyridōn plērōmata klasmatōn ērate? — when the seven for the four thousand, how many baskets of fragments did you pick up? — the basket-word changes: spyridas (vs. kophinous in v.19) matching the actual accounts (6:43 kophinus; 8:8 spyridas). The disciples' correct answers (hepta = seven) prove they remember the factual details; what they lack is the capacity to interpret them. The One who fed thousands twice is in the boat with them — bread-anxiety is incoherent.
8.21: kai elegen autois, Oupō suniete? — and he said to them: do you not yet understand? — the pericope ends on an open question, not a declaration. suniemi (to understand, to put it together — the capacity to synthesize evidence into comprehension; used for the disciples' failure throughout: 4:13; 6:52; 7:18) is the word for what the two feedings, correctly interpreted, should have produced. The question hangs: the answer is expected of the reader as much as the disciples.
8.22: Kai erchontai eis Bēthsaida, kai pherosin autō tuphlon kai parakalosin auton hina autou hapsētai — and they came to Bethsaida, and they brought him a blind man and begged him to touch him — Bethsaida (on the northeastern shore of Galilee, Philip's tetrarchy) is outside Jewish Galilee. The healing-by-touch request (hapsētai) echoes the hemorrhaging woman's approach (5:27). The blind man is brought by others — the same pattern as the Syrophoenician daughter (7:25) and the deaf-mute (7:32).
8.23: kai epilabomenos tēs cheiros tou typhlou exēnenken auton exō tēs kōmēs — and taking the blind man by the hand he led him outside the village — the withdrawal to privacy before healing (cf. 7:33 taking the deaf-mute apart from the crowd). Jesus leads the blind man by the hand — physical guidance as well as healing. ptysas eis ta ommata autou (having spat into his eyes) — spittle applied directly to the eyes, a physical healing medium consistent with 7:33. kai epitheis tas cheiras autō epērōta auton, Ei ti blepeis? — and having laid hands on him he asked him: do you see anything?
8.24: kai anablepas elegen, Blepō tous anthrōpous hoti hōs dendra horō peripatountas — and looking up he said: I see the people, for as trees I see them walking — partial sight: the people are visible but unfocused, tree-sized and indistinct. This is the only Markan miracle requiring two applications; the first laying-on-hands produces incomplete healing. The structural positioning is significant: the two-stage healing follows the disciples' two-stage understanding (they know who Jesus is — v.29 — but cannot yet grasp the cross), anticipating that full sight will require a second encounter with Christ's person and work.
8.25: eita palin epethēken tas cheiras epi tous ophtalmous autou, kai dieblepen kai apekatestē kai eneblepsen tēlaugōs apanta — then again he laid hands on his eyes, and he looked intently and was restored and saw everything clearly — apekatestē (was restored completely — aorist passive of apokathistēmi = to reconstitute, restore fully; the same root as apokatastasis, the complete restoration of all things) and tēlaugōs (clearly, at a visible distance — the adverb of far-shining clarity) describe the completed state: full restoration of clear long-distance vision. The progression from no sight to partial sight to complete sight maps the spiritual journey that will continue through the next section of the Gospel.
8.26: kai apesteilen auton eis oikon autou legōn, Mēde eis tēn kōmēn eiselthēs — and he sent him to his home saying: do not even enter the village — the Messianic Secret extended: not merely silence-commanded but direction-given (eis oikon = to your home). The healed man is sent back to domestic space, not into public space where news would spread. The restriction matches 5:19 (the Gerasene told to go to his house) and 7:29–30 (the Syrophoenician mother goes home to find her daughter healed).
8.27: kai en tē hodō epērōta tous mathētas autou legōn autois, Tina me legousin hoi anthrōpoi einai? — and on the road he was asking his disciples saying to them: who do the people say that I am? — the Caesarea Philippi question is placed at Mark's structural midpoint: chapters 1–8 are the Galilean ministry of power; chapters 9–16 are the journey to the cross. The question on the road (en tē hodō) anticipates the journey-to-Jerusalem that will define the rest of the narrative. The popular identifications (v.28) all cast Jesus as a prophetic figure — John the Baptist, Elijah, one of the prophets — none identifies him as the Messiah.
8.28: hoi de eipon autō legontes hoti Iōannēn ton Baptistēn, kai alloi Elian, alloi de hoti heis tōn prophētōn — they said: John the Baptist; others: Elijah; and others: one of the prophets — the three identifications map the prophetic horizon of expectation. The John the Baptist identification echoes Herod's fearful supposition (6:14,16). The Elijah identification connects to Malachi 4:5 (Elijah will return before the great Day of the Lord) — a widespread Second Temple expectation. The generic prophetic category casts Jesus in the tradition of the great OT spokespersons. None of these captures his identity; all project a known category onto him.
8.30: kai epetimēsen autois hina mēdeni legōsin peri autou — and he charged them that they should tell no one about him — the Messianic Secret command follows the only correct identification. The command is now absolute and immediate: the correct confession (su ei ho Christos) triggers not proclamation but silence. The silence command makes the passion-prediction that immediately follows (v.31) the authorized context for understanding what kind of Messiah he is; without the cross, the confession is incomplete.
8.31: kai ērxato didaskein autous hoti dei ton huion tou anthrōpou polla pathein kai apodokimasthēnai hypo tōn presbyterōn kai tōn archiereōn kai tōn grammateōn kai apoktanthēnai kai meta treis hēmeras anastēnai — and he began to teach them that it is necessary for the Son of Man to suffer many things and to be rejected by the elders and the chief priests and the scribes and to be killed and after three days to rise — dei (it is necessary, it is divinely obligatory — the word for divine necessity throughout Luke-Acts, John, and the NT passion-predictions) is the key term: the suffering is not accidental, permitted, or merely foreseen — it is divinely ordained. The three-part Sanhedrin (presbyteroi = elders; archiereis = chief priests; grammateis = scribes) is the complete Jerusalem authority that will execute the condemnation. Meta treis hēmeras anastēnai (to rise after three days) is stated as clearly as the rejection and death.
8.32: kai parrēsia ton logon elalei — and plainly/openly he was speaking the word — parrēsia (frank speech, bold openness — the opposite of veiled or cryptic communication; the speech of one who holds back nothing). The passion-prediction is stated with maximum clarity to the disciples, contrasting with the parable-speech of chapter 4 which concealed from outsiders. kai proslabomenos ho Petros auton ērxato epitiman autō — and Peter taking him aside began to rebuke him — epitimaō (to rebuke, to charge sternly) is the word Jesus uses for rebuking demons (1:25; 3:12) and storms (4:39); Peter uses it against Jesus. The inversion is the narrative shock of the scene.
8.33: ho de epistrapheis kai idōn tous mathētas autou epetimēsen Petrō kai legei, Hypage opisō mou, Satana, hoti ou phroneis ta tou theou alla ta tōn anthrōpōn — and turning and seeing his disciples he rebuked Peter and says: get behind me, Satan, because you do not think the things of God but the things of men — phroneis (you think/are minded — from phroneō = to set one's mind on, to be oriented toward; opposite of tou theou phronein that Paul calls the Spirit's mindset in Rom 8:5–6). ta tou theou vs. ta tōn anthrōpōn — the divine vs. human perspective on the Messiah is absolute: the divine plan includes the cross; the human perspective is repelled by it. The identification of Peter's protest with "Satan" (Satana) links this scene with 1:13 and 8:11 — resistance to the cross is Satanic at the structural level, regardless of the speaker's intent.
8.35: hos gar ean thelē tēn psychēn autou sōsai apolesei autēn, hos d' an apolese tēn psychēn autou heneken emou kai tou euangeliou sōsei autēn — for whoever wants to save his soul/life will lose it; but whoever loses his soul/life for my sake and the gospel will save it — psychē (soul/life — the single Greek word covering biological life and the eternal person; the polysemy is essential to the saying). The saying operates simultaneously on two levels: physical life (saved by denying Christ = ultimately lost; surrendered for Christ = ultimately saved) and eternal life (the same logic at a deeper level). The double-level meaning is irreducible; neither level cancels the other.
8.36: ti gar ōphelei anthrōpon kerdēsai ton kosmon holon kai zēmiōthēnai tēn psychēn autou? — what does it profit a man to gain the whole world and to suffer loss in respect of his soul? — kerdainō (to gain profit — commercial vocabulary for profitable transactions) and zēmioō (to suffer loss, to be penalized — the corresponding loss-term; used in the papyri for fines) frame the calculation as a commercial exchange. The whole world on one side; the psychē on the other. The calculation is impossible: no sum of material acquisition compensates for the forfeiture of the person.
8.37: ti gar dōsei anthrōpos antallagma tēs psychēs autou? — for what will a man give as an exchange for his soul? — antallagma (exchange, a compensating value — from anti-allassō = to exchange in place of) appears in Job 28:15 LXX where no gold or silver can be given as antallagma for wisdom. The rhetorical form is a second unanswerable question: there is no antallagma, no possible counterweight for the psychē. The soul's value exceeds any convertible equivalent. The commercial metaphor is completed: no bargain recovers what is lost when the soul is forfeited.
8.38: hos gar ean epaischynthē me kai tous emous logous en tē genea tautē tē moichalidi kai hamartōlō, kai ho huios tou anthrōpou epaischynthēsetai auton — for whoever is ashamed of me and my words in this adulterous and sinful generation, the Son of Man will be ashamed of him — epaischynō (to be ashamed of, to feel the disgrace of association with — the verb of public dissociation in an honor-shame culture). In the first-century Mediterranean world, to be "ashamed of" someone is to publicly distance oneself from them to preserve one's own honor-standing. The Christological demand is stated as an inverse: if you deny the Messiah before humans to preserve social honor, the Son of Man will deny you at the judgment. hotan elthē en tē doxē tou patros autou meta tōn angelōn tōn hagiōn — when he comes in the glory of his Father with the holy angels — the coming-in-glory is the Daniel 7:13 enthronement event (cf. 13:26; 14:62); the holy angels attend the cosmic judgment scene (cf. Matt 25:31). The Son of Man who now walks to the cross will come in the Father's glory: the full reversal of the current hiddenness.

9.24: pisteuō; boethei mou te apistia — I believe; help my unbelief — one of the most theologically honest statements in the Gospels; the father's response is not a claim to complete faith but a simultaneous affirmation of what faith he has and an acknowledgment of its inadequacy. The prayer is self-aware about its own condition: the faith that asks for help with unbelief is already a form of faith (it turns to Christ rather than away from him). The raw honesty is characteristic of Mark's straightforward Christological realism.
9.1: amēn legō hymin hoti eisin tines tōn hōde hestēkotōn oitines ou mē geusōntai thanatou heōs an idōsin tēn basileian tou theou elēlythuian en dynamei — truly I tell you that there are some of those standing here who will not taste death until they see the kingdom of God having come in power — the saying has generated significant interpretive debate. Three main referents for the kingdom coming in power: (1) the Transfiguration immediately following (vv.2–8); (2) the Resurrection and Pentecost; (3) the destruction of Jerusalem (AD 70). The Markan placement immediately before the Transfiguration supports reading it as a partial fulfillment — a preview of the Son of Man coming in the glory of the Father (8:38). en dynamei (in power) is the qualifier: not the kingdom's veiled presence in Jesus's current ministry but its manifest, undeniable expression.
9.2: kai metamorphōthē emprosthen autōn — and he was transfigured before them — metamorphoō (to transform in form — the source of the English "metamorphosis") in the passive (metamorphōthē, divine passive — he was transfigured by divine action) marks this as a revelation given, not a self-display. The form (morphē) becoming visible is the glory Jesus possesses as Son of God, momentarily uncloaked before three witnesses. The three (Peter, James, John) are the inner circle who will also witness the Gethsemane agony (14:33): the same three see the glory and the grief. meta hēmeras hex (after six days) is the precise temporal note; Luke 9:28 says "about eight days" — the difference may reflect inclusive vs. exclusive counting.
9.3: kai ta himatia autou egeneto stilbonta leuka lian, hoia gnapheys epi tēs gēs ou dynatai houtōs leukanai — and his garments became intensely gleaming white, such as a bleacher on earth cannot so whiten — stilbō (to flash, gleam, glitter — of radiant light) and leuka (white) together describe a luminosity beyond earthly process. The comparison to a fuller/bleacher (gnapheys, one who washes and whitens cloth) that cannot achieve the same result establishes the otherworldly quality. Daniel 7:9 describes the Ancient of Days's garment as "white as snow"; Revelation 1:14 describes Christ's hair similarly. The garments' transformation is the visible index of the divine glory being revealed.
9.4: kai ōphthē autois Ēlias syn Mōysei kai ēsan syllalountes tō Iēsou — and there appeared to them Elijah with Moses and they were talking with Jesus — ōphthē (appeared — aorist passive of horaō; the same verb used for resurrection appearances in 1 Cor 15:5–8) marks the visionary quality. Mark names Elijah before Moses — the eschatological order (Elijah as forerunner of the Day of the Lord; Malachi 4:5) rather than the chronological order. The two figures represent the prophets and the law — together encompassing the entirety of OT revelation, now converging on the One they both anticipated. Luke 9:31 specifies they spoke of Jesus's exodos — his departure/death in Jerusalem.
9.5: Rhabbi, kalon estin hēmas hōde einai, kai poiēsōmen treis skēnas, soi mian kai Mōysei mian kai Ēlia mian — Rabbi, it is good for us to be here; let us make three shelters — one for you, one for Moses and one for Elijah — Rhabbi (Teacher — Aramaic title for a recognized instructor) is Peter's address when he does not know how to respond. skēnē (tent, booth, tabernacle — the word of both the Feast of Tabernacles/Sukkot and the Wilderness Tabernacle housing the divine presence) may suggest a Sukkot-festival association, or simply that Peter wants to prolong the encounter. Three equal booths places Moses and Elijah on the same level as Jesus — a category-error that the voice from the cloud immediately corrects.
9.6: ou gar ēdei ti apokrithē, ekphoboi gar egenonto — for he did not know what to say, for they were terrified — Mark's frank admission of Peter's incomprehension: ēdei (he knew — imperfect, he was not knowing, could not find what to say). ekphobos (intensely afraid, out-of-one's-mind with fear — ek- + phobos = extreme fear) is the theophanic fear-response, not a mild unease. Peter's suggestion is offered out of incomprehension and dread. The verse is characteristic of Mark's honest portrayal of the disciples' limitations at moments of divine revelation.
9.7: kai egeneto nephelē episkiazousa autois, kai egeneto phōnē ek tēs nephelēs — and a cloud came overshadowing them, and a voice came from the cloud — nephelē (cloud) is the theophanic vehicle throughout the OT: the pillar of cloud in the wilderness (Exod 13:21), the cloud of glory on Sinai (Exod 19:9; 24:15–18), the Shekinah-cloud filling the Temple (1 Kgs 8:10–11). The divine presence arrives via the same vehicle as Sinai. Houtos estin ho huios mou ho agapētos, akouete autou — this is my beloved Son; listen to him — the declaration matches the baptism-voice (1:11) but adds akouete autou (listen to him): a citation of Deuteronomy 18:15 LXX (autou akousesthe = to him you shall listen), where Moses promised a coming prophet. The voice identifies Jesus as the eschatological Prophet of Deuteronomy 18 — the definitive speaker of divine words, superseding Moses himself.
9.8: kai exapina periblepsamennoi ouketi oudena eidon alla ton Iēsoun monon meth' heautōn — and suddenly looking around they no longer saw anyone except Jesus alone with them — exapina (suddenly, all at once) marks the abrupt end of the vision. Moses and Elijah have withdrawn; only Jesus remains. monon (alone, only) is the theological emphasis: Jesus alone remains as the one to hear and to follow. The Law (Moses) and the Prophets (Elijah) have been subsumed into the Son — the earlier modes of revelation have yielded to the final Word.
9.9: kai katabaiontes ek tou orous diesteilato autois hina mēdeni ha eidon diēgēsōntai, ei mē hotan ho huios tou anthrōpou ek nekrōn anastē — and as they came down from the mountain he charged them to tell no one what they had seen until the Son of Man rose from the dead — the Messianic Secret with a defined termination point: the silence is not permanent but until (heōs) the resurrection. ek nekrōn anastē (rose from the dead — standard resurrection formula) is stated plainly as the future event that will lift the prohibition. The Transfiguration cannot be correctly understood before the resurrection: a visionary preview of glory without the death that precedes it would be read as triumphalism rather than vindication-after-suffering.
9.10: kai ton logon ekratēsan pros heautous syzētountes ti estin to ek nekrōn anastēnai — and they kept the matter among themselves, debating what the rising from the dead meant — ekratēsan ton logon (held the matter/command to themselves — krateō = to grip, hold firmly) alongside syzētountes (debating together — iterative, ongoing discussion). The specific confusion is about ek nekrōn anastēnai (rising from among the dead). Jewish expectation had a general resurrection at the end of the age; the notion of a single individual rising before that general event was outside the standard framework. The disciples do not have a category for what Jesus has predicted.
9.11: kai epērōtōn auton legontes hoti legousin hoi grammateis hoti Ēlian dei elthein prōton — and they were asking him: why do the scribes say that Elijah must come first — the Malachi 4:5 expectation ("I will send you Elijah the prophet before the great and awesome day of the LORD") was standard scribal teaching. The disciples' question connects the Elijah-appearance on the mountain with the Elijah-must-come-first doctrine: if Elijah appeared but the Day of the LORD has not arrived, what does it mean? The question reflects genuine theological perplexity, not hostility.
9.12: Ho de ephē autois, Ēlias men elthōn prōton apokathistanei panta — and he said to them: Elijah indeed comes first and restores all things — Jesus affirms the Elijah-must-come-first doctrine. apokathistanei panta (restores all things — present tense, the settled expectation) uses the full restoration-word: apokathistēmi (to restore to the original state — apo- = back + kathistēmi = to establish; cf. Acts 3:21). kai pōs gegraptai epi ton huion tou anthrōpou hina polla pathē kai exoudēnōthē — and how is it written about the Son of Man that he must suffer many things and be treated with contempt — the counterquestion: the Son of Man's script in Scripture includes rejection and suffering. exoudēnoō (to treat as nothing — from ex- + ouden = to reduce to nothing, to despise utterly) is the word for total contempt, echoing the Suffering Servant language of Isaiah 53:3 LXX.
9.13: alla legō hymin hoti kai Ēlias elēlythen, kai epoiēsan autō hosa ēthelon, kathōs gegraptai ep' auton — but I tell you that Elijah has also come, and they did to him whatever they wanted, as it is written about him — the identification of John the Baptist as the fulfillment of the Elijah expectation (explicit in Matthew 11:14; 17:13; implicit here). elēlythen (has come — perfect: the arrival is complete and the result stands). hosa ēthelon (whatever they wanted) parallels Ahab and Jezebel's persecution of Elijah (1 Kgs 19:2,10) — John's imprisonment and beheading by Herod at Herodias's instigation maps onto the Elijah-Jezebel template. The Elijah-type has been fulfilled and rejected simultaneously.
9.14: Kai elthontes pros tous mathētas eidon ochlon polun peri autous kai grammateis syzētountas pros autous — and coming to the other disciples they saw a large crowd around them and scribes debating with them — the descent from the mountain's divine glory into the valley's controversy and failure is abrupt and structurally deliberate. On the mountain: the Father's declaration and divine presence; in the valley: scribal challenge and disciples' inability. The structural juxtaposition is Markan theology in narrative form: the glory of the Son is not yet accessible; the disciples operate in the struggle of the present age.
9.15: kai euthys pas ho ochlos idontes auton exethambethesan kai prostrechontes ēspazontos auton — and immediately all the crowd seeing him were overwhelmed with amazement and running to him they greeted him — ekthambeō (to be intensely amazed, overwhelmed — a strong compound emotion-word used at 1:27; 10:24,32; 16:5,6) is the crowd's immediate response to Jesus's appearance. Some commentators propose that residual radiance from the Transfiguration explains the unusual amazement; Mark does not say so, leaving the reaction to the reader's inference.
9.16: kai epērōtēsen autous, Ti syzēteite pros autous? — and he asked them: what are you debating with them? — the open question invites the public account of the failed exorcism (vv.17–18). The disciples' failure has become a public matter debated with the scribes rather than a private learning moment; the question exposes it before the crowd and sets up the teaching that follows.
9.17: kai apekrithē autō heis ek tou ochlou, Didaskale, ēnenka ton huion mou pros se, echonta pneuma alalon — and one from the crowd answered him: Teacher, I brought my son to you, having a mute spirit — pneuma alalon (a mute/speechless spirit — alalos = without speech, speechless) identifies the possessing spirit by its chief symptom. Verse 25 will add kōphon (deaf) to the description. The father frames the case as: my son has a spirit — the demonic agency is the cause; the boy is the victim. Didaskale (Teacher) is the crowd-address; the father does not yet call Jesus "Lord" (that comes in v.24's neighbor-verse, v.22).
9.18: kai hopou ean auton katalabē rēssei auton, kai aphrizei kai trizei tous odontas kai xērainetai — and wherever it seizes him it throws him down, and he foams and gnashes his teeth and becomes rigid — the triad of clinical symptoms: rēssō (to throw down, convulse violently), aphrizō (to foam at the mouth — aphros = foam), trizō tous odontas (to grind/gnash the teeth), xērainō (to become dry/rigid — the same verb as the withered hand of 3:1). The symptom-portrait resembles what would be diagnosed as tonic-clonic seizure; the Markan narrative does not separate the demonological and medical dimensions but treats the spiritual reality as the underlying cause. kai eipa tois mathētais sou hina auto ekbalōsin, kai ouk ischysan — and I told your disciples to cast it out and they were not strong enough.
9.19: Ho de apokritheis autois legei, Ō genea apistos, heōs pote pros hymas esomai? heōs pote anexomai hymōn? — and answering he says to them: O unbelieving generation, how long shall I be with you? how long shall I put up with you? — genea apistos (faithless generation — the alpha-privative of pistos, faithful/trusting) is the diagnosis. The double heōs pote (how long?) echoes the wilderness-complaint texts where God laments the faithless people (Num 14:27 LXX: heōs pote tēn genean tautēn tēn ponēran). Jesus applies the Exodus-generation judgment to his contemporaries. The lament is genuine: the one who has repeatedly demonstrated divine power is surrounded by persistent unbelief.
9.20: kai ēnenkan auton pros auton, kai idōn auton to pneuma euthys synesparaxen auton, kai pesōn epi tēs gēs ekulieto aphrizōn — and they brought him to him, and seeing him the spirit immediately convulsed him, and falling on the ground he was rolling and foaming — euthys; the spirit's response to Jesus's presence is immediate and violent before any command is given. sysprassō (to convulse/tear together — the intensive compound) and kuliō (to roll, writhe) describe the extreme seizure at the moment of encounter. The violence at the sight of Jesus (not in response to a command) is the demonic counterpart of involuntary submission recorded in 1:24 and 5:6–7: the unclean spirits recognize Jesus's authority and react with either obeisance or violence.
9.21: kai epērōtēsen ton patera autou, Possos chronos estin hōs touto gegonen autō? Ho de eipen, Ek paidiothen — and he asked his father: how long has this been happening to him? and he said: from childhood — possos chronos (how long a time — chronological duration) establishes the depth of the entrapment: not a recent onset but from infancy (paidiothen = from the time of being a small child). The pastoral attention to chronological detail is characteristic of Mark's healing accounts (5:25: twelve years; 5:42: twelve years old). Jesus's interest in the history personalizes the encounter rather than treating the case as a generic exorcism.
9.22: kai pollakis kai eis pyr auton ebalen kai eis hydata hina apolesei auton — and many times it threw him into fire and water to destroy him — pollakis (many times, repeatedly) emphasizes the chronic destruction-attempts. The demonic intent is explicit: ina apolesei auton (in order to destroy him — apollymi = to destroy utterly). all' ei ti dynē, boēthēson hēmin splagchnistheis eph' hēmas — but if you can do anything, have compassion on us and help us — ei ti dynē (if you are able to do anything) is the conditional that challenges Jesus's power: the father has seen the disciples fail and qualified his faith accordingly. splagchnizomai is the gut-compassion appeal (cf. 1:41; 6:34; 8:2). hēmin (on us) — the father identifies himself with his son: the suffering is shared.
9.23: Ho de Iēsous eipen autō, To ei dynē — panta dynata tō pisteuonti — and Jesus said to him: the "if you can" — all things are possible for the one who believes — Jesus echoes the father's conditional (ei ti dynē) back as a quoted phrase with an implicit incredulity: to ei dynē (the "if you can" — you are really framing this as a question about my capacity?). The capacity-question is redirected to a faith-question. panta dynata tō pisteuonti (all things are possible for the one who believes) is not a general faith-formula for any petition but a specific statement that the obstacle in this encounter is not Jesus's power but the presence or absence of faith that opens the work of God.
9.25: idōn de ho Iēsous hoti episyntrechei ochlos, epetimēsen tō pneumati tō akathartō legōn autō, To alalon kai kōphon pneuma, egō soi epitassō, exelthe ex autou kai mēketi eiselthēs eis auton — seeing that a crowd was running together, he rebuked the unclean spirit saying: you mute and deaf spirit, I command you, come out of him and never enter him again — Jesus addresses the spirit by its symptoms: alalon kai kōphon (mute and deaf). egō soi epitassō (I command you — the first-person emphatic egō + epitassō = to command with authority). The prohibition is permanent: mēketi eiselthēs eis auton (never enter him again) — this distinguishes the healing from cases where re-entry was possible; the cure is final.
9.26: kai kraxas kai polla sparaxas exēlthen, kai egeneto hōsei nekros, hōste tous pollous legein hoti apethanen — and crying out and greatly convulsing him it came out, and he became as if dead, so that most said he had died — the exit is violent: kraxas (the same exiting-shriek as 1:26; 5:7) and polla sparaxas (convulsing greatly). The post-exorcism collapse looks terminal to onlookers — hōsei nekros (as if dead). The condition creates a death-and-resurrection pattern: the boy who appeared dead will be raised by Jesus's hand in v.27, enacting the resurrection-pattern in miniature and anticipating the passion-resurrection predictions framing this scene.
9.27: ho de Iēsous kratēsas tēs cheiros autou ēgeiren auton, kai anestē — but Jesus taking him by the hand lifted him up, and he stood up — krateō tēs cheiros (taking by the hand) is the resurrection-gesture: the same action as raising Jairus's daughter (5:41: ekratēsen tēs cheiros tou paidiou). ēgeiren (lifted up, raised — the standard resurrection-verb egeirō) and anestē (stood up — the root of anastasis, resurrection). The sequence — collapsed as if dead → raised by the hand of Jesus → stood up — enacts the resurrection in miniature at the structural center of a chapter bounded by passion-predictions (8:31; 9:31).
9.28: Kai eiselthontos autou eis oikon hoi mathētai autou kat' idian epērōtōn auton, Hoti hēmeis ouk ēdynasthēmen ekbalein auto? — and when he had entered a house his disciples privately asked him: why were we not able to cast it out? — the house/crowd spatial pattern again (cf. 7:17; 4:10). The disciples' question assumes they had genuine delegated authority to exorcise (consistent with 6:7,13 where they were sent out and succeeded), and that this failure therefore requires explanation. The failure is not presented as routine.
9.29: kai eipen autois, Touto to genos en oudeni dynatai exelthein ei mē en proseuchē — and he said to them: this kind can come out by nothing except prayer — touto to genos (this kind/genus) implies a taxonomy of spiritual powers: some categories require specific conditions. proseuchē (prayer — directed communication toward God) alone is named; the UBS5/NA28 text does not include "and fasting" (some MSS add kai nēsteia as a secondary expansion). The implication: the disciples attempted the exorcism relying on delegated authority as a formula, without maintained communion with God through prayer. Authority over this category of demonic resistance requires ongoing dependence on God, not merely the use of Jesus's name.
9.30: Kakeithen exelthontes paraporeuonto dia tēs Galilaias, kai ouk ēthelen hina tis gnō — and leaving from there they were passing through Galilee, and he did not want anyone to know — the concealed Galilean passage is explicitly in service of teaching the disciples (edidasken gar tous mathētas autou = for he was teaching his disciples, v.31). The private teaching-journey mode is the deliberate counterpart to the public ministry of chapters 1–6. The content of the private teaching is the second passion prediction.
9.31: edidasken gar tous mathētas autou kai elegen autois hoti Ho huios tou anthrōpou paradidotai eis cheiras anthrōpōn, kai apoktanousin auton, kai apoktantheis meta treis hēmeras anastēsetai — for he was teaching his disciples and saying to them: the Son of Man is being delivered into the hands of men, and they will kill him, and having been killed after three days he will rise — paradidotai (is being delivered, betrayed — present tense, not future) frames the betrayal as a process already in motion. The passive marks Jesus as delivered rather than merely captured: someone is handing him over. eis cheiras anthrōpōn (into the hands of men) — the Son of Man delivered into human hands is the inversion of Daniel 7:13–14 where the Son of Man receives authority and all peoples are delivered into his hands. The three days are stated plainly: death is not the end of the prediction.
9.32: hoi de ēgnooun to rhēma kai ephobounto auton epērōtēsai — and they did not understand the saying and were afraid to ask him — ēgnooun (were ignorant of, did not comprehend — imperfect of agnoeō = not to know, the opposite of gnōsis) describes continuing incomprehension. The added detail: ephobounto auton epērōtēsai (they were afraid to ask him) — the fear prevents the clarifying question. They understand enough to be unsettled and not enough to process; fear seals the incomprehension rather than opening it to resolution. The disciples' trajectory from Caesarea Philippi through Gethsemane is one of increasing incomprehension and fear.
9.33: Kai ēlthon eis Kapharnaoun, kai en tē oikia genomenos epērōta autous, Ti en tē hodō dielogizesthe? — and they came to Capernaum, and being in the house he asked them: what were you debating on the road? — Capernaum is the Galilean base (1:21; 2:1). The house-setting is the private teaching space. dielogizesthe (were you reasoning/debating together — the same root as dialogismos, 2:6,8; 8:16) is Jesus's probing question: the subject is not volunteered; he asks directly. The connection to vv.34–35 is that the disciples' road-debate about greatness is placed in proximity to Jesus's passion-prediction — the juxtaposition is the verdict.
9.34: hoi de esiōpōn, pros allēlous gar dielechthēsan en tē hodō tis meizōn — and they were silent, for on the road they had argued with one another who was greatest — esiōpōn (they were silent — imperfect: sustained silence, the silence of shame). tis meizōn (who is greater) was the subject: a greatness-ranking argument. The juxtaposition is devastating: Jesus predicts his own death and delivery into human hands; the disciples debate their own relative precedence. The silence is not ignorance but shame — they recognize the incongruity they cannot verbalize.
9.35: kai kathisas ephōnēsen tous dōdeka kai legei autois, Ei tis thelei prōtos einai estai pantōn eschatos kai pantōn diakonos — and sitting down he called the Twelve and says to them: if anyone wants to be first he will be last of all and servant of all — kathisas (having sat — the posture of authoritative teaching; cf. 13:3). diakonos (servant, table-server — later the term for the church office of deacon, derived from this and parallel teaching) is the inversion: the path to greatness runs through service, not rank. pantōn eschatos (last of all) and pantōn diakonos (servant of all) — the superlative-absolute forms: not "less great" but last and servant of everyone.
9.36: kai labōn paidion estēsen auto en mesō autōn kai enagkalisamenos auto eipen autois — and taking a little child he placed it in the middle of them and taking it in his arms he said to them — the embodied teaching: paidion (small child) is placed en mesō autōn (in the middle of them) as the visual object-lesson for the verbal teaching of v.35. enagkalizomai (to take in the arms, to embrace — used only here and 10:16 in the NT) is the action that accompanies the teaching: Jesus holds the child while speaking. The embrace embodies what he is teaching about reception of the insignificant.
9.37: hos an hen tōn toioutōn paidion dexētai epi tō onomati mou, eme dechetai, kai hos an eme dexētai, ouk eme dechetai alla ton aposteilanta me — whoever receives one of these little children in my name receives me; and whoever receives me does not receive me but the one who sent me — dechomai (to receive, welcome — the hospitality-reception verb). epi tō onomati mou (in my name — because they belong to me, on the basis of my identity). The chain: receiving the insignificant child = receiving Jesus = receiving the Father. The logic inverts the honor hierarchy: the socially invisible and powerless child is the site of encounter with God. The "sent" language (ton aposteilanta me) is the Johannine mission-chain (cf. John 13:20): to receive the sent-one is to receive the Sender.
9.38: Ephē autō ho Iōannēs, Didaskale, eidomen tina en tō onomati sou ekballonta daimonia, kai ekōlyomen auton, hoti ouk ēkolouthei hēmin — John said to him: Teacher, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we tried to stop him, because he was not following us — the motivation is explicit: hoti ouk ēkolouthei hēmin (because he was not following us — not "following you" but us). The disciples have made group-membership their criterion for valid ministry. The irony is structural: they have just failed to cast out a demon (v.18); this unaffiliated person succeeded in the same activity. Their failure generates a boundary-policing attempt; Jesus's response releases the boundary.
9.39: Ho de Iēsous eipen, Mē kōluete auton. Oudeis gar estin hos poiēsei dynamin epi tō onomati mou kai dynēsetai tachy kakologēsai me — and Jesus said: do not stop him. For no one who does a miracle in my name will be able soon to speak evil of me — mē kōluete (do not stop — the same verb the disciples used for their prohibition). The reason is psychological-theological: the use of Jesus's name in effective miracle-working creates an alignment with Jesus that makes immediate apostasy (kakologēsai me = to speak evil of me) logically incompatible with the act just performed. Effective ministry in Jesus's name creates a real if informal connection to him.
9.40: hos gar ouk estin kath' hēmōn, hyper hēmōn estin — for whoever is not against us is for us — the positive boundary: it is drawn at opposition, not formal discipleship. The saying stands in apparent tension with Matthew 12:30 / Luke 11:23 ("whoever is not with me is against me") but the contexts differ: Matthew 12 addresses explicit opposition to Jesus's exorcistic work; Mark 9 addresses independent ministry using Jesus's name. The criterion here is: absence of active opposition = at least implicitly aligned.
9.41: Hos gar an potisē hymas potērion hydatos en onomati hoti Christou este, amēn legō hymin hoti ou mē apolese ton misthon autou — for whoever gives you a cup of water in the name that you are Christ's, truly I tell you he will certainly not lose his reward — potērion hydatos (a cup of water — the minimum possible act of hospitality, a single cup). en onomati hoti Christou este (in the name/identity that you are of Christ — because the recipients bear the identity of belonging to the Messiah). The amēn oath-formula guarantees: no act of service toward those who belong to Christ goes unrewarded, however small. The saying follows directly from the child-in-the-middle teaching: even the cup of water given to the insignificant disciple counts.
9.42: Kai hos an skandalisē hena tōn mikrōn toutōn tōn pisteuontōn, kalon estin autō mallon ei perikeite mylos onikos peri ton trachēlon autou kai beblētai eis tēn thalassan — and whoever causes one of these little ones who believe to stumble, it is better for him if a large millstone were placed around his neck and he were thrown into the sea — skandalizō (to cause to stumble, to trip someone into sin or loss of faith — from skandalon = the trigger of a trap). hoi mikroi ... hoi pisteuontes (the little ones who believe — the vulnerable believers, those without social standing) are the protected group. mylos onikos (donkey-millstone — the large upper millstone of a donkey-driven mill, enormously heavy vs. the small hand-millstone) around the neck and drowning: the lesser fate compared to what awaits those who destroy the faith of the vulnerable.
9.43: Kai ean skandalizē se hē cheir sou, apokoson autēn — and if your hand causes you to stumble, cut it off — the escalating series (hand → foot → eye) uses the most radical physical imagery for the absolute seriousness of not allowing anything to impede entry into life. apokoson (cut off — decisive aorist imperative of apokoptō). The hyperbole is rhetorical rather than prescriptive — the OT never commanded literal self-mutilation — but the exaggeration makes the point: better to enter life without the offending limb than to enter Gehenna with all parts intact. hē zōē (life) vs. geenna (Gehenna) are the two destinies.
9.44: Verse 44 is absent in the best manuscripts (including Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, and Codex Washingtonianus) and is regarded by UBS5/NA28 as a scribal interpolation of the refrain from v.48. The phrase hopou ho skōlēx autōn ou teleutā kai to pyr ou sbennyetai (where their worm does not die and the fire is not quenched) is the Isaiah 66:24 citation; a copyist repeated it after v.43 to complete the rhetorical pattern, though the original text placed it only at v.48.
9.45: kai ean ho pous sou skandalizē se, apokoson auton — and if your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off — the foot continues the series: hand (the instrument of doing), foot (the instrument of going), eye (v.47, the instrument of seeing and desiring). The three members represent different modes of engagement in sin; each is named with the same absolute counsel. kalon estin eiselthein eis tēn zōēn chōlon ē tous duo podas echonta blēthēnai eis tēn geennan — better to enter life lame than having two feet to be thrown into Gehenna.
9.46: As with v.44, v.46 is absent in the best manuscript tradition and represents a scribal repetition of the v.48 refrain after v.45 to maintain the three-part rhetorical structure. UBS5/NA28 omits it. The Isaiah 66:24 citation belongs at v.48 where it closes the Gehenna-sayings as a whole.
9.47: kai ean ho ophthalmos sou skandalizē se, ekbale auton — and if your eye causes you to stumble, pluck it out — the third member: ekbale auton (pluck it out — the same verb as "cast out" for exorcisms; ekballō applied to one's own eye). The eye as the organ of desire and envy (ophthalmos ponēros = evil eye, 7:22) is the final, most intimate member of the series. monophthalmon eiselthein eis tēn basileian tou theou ē duo ophtalmous echonta blēthēnai eis tēn geennan — the positive destination here is explicitly hē basileia tou theou (the kingdom of God), the term used throughout chapters 1–10 for the positive eschatological state.
9.48: hopou ho skōlēx autōn ou teleutā kai to pyr ou sbennyetai — where their worm does not die and the fire is not quenched — the citation is Isaiah 66:24, the closing verse of Isaiah: "they will go out and see the dead bodies of those who rebelled against me; for their worm will not die and their fire will not be quenched." geenna (Gehenna) is the Greek form of Hebrew Gē-Hinnom (Valley of Hinnom), the ravine south of Jerusalem associated with child-sacrifice to Molech (2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31) and later used as the city's refuse dump with its perpetual fires. Jesus uses Gehenna as his consistent image for final judgment, and the Isaiah 66:24 citation — the last verse of the OT's longest prophetic book — provides the authoritative scriptural warrant. The worm and the fire are both inextinguishable: permanent, not temporary.
9.49: pās gar pyri halisthēsetai — for everyone will be salted with fire — among the most difficult sayings in Mark. The three main interpretations: (1) the purifying trial of persecution and suffering is the "salt" that preserves and refines every disciple — fire as purifying agent (cf. Mal 3:2; 1 Pet 1:7); (2) the Gehenna-fire of vv.43–48 will "salt" those who are judged — judgment as the terrible analogue of preservation; (3) every sacrifice was required to be salted (Lev 2:13: "every offering of grain shall be seasoned with salt") — the disciples' lives as sacrificial offerings salted with the fire of suffering for Christ. The connection between fire (vv.43–48) and salt (vv.49–50) is the verbal bridge; the full semantic connection remains debated across the tradition.
9.50: Kalon to halas — ean de to halas analon genētai, en tini auto artyesete? — salt is good — but if the salt becomes saltless, with what will you season it? — halas (salt) carries multiple registers: preservation (against decay), covenant (berit melach, the covenant of salt — Lev 2:13; Num 18:19), and quality/flavor. analon (without salt, saltless — the alpha-privative of halas) describes salt that has lost its defining property: the rhetorical question has no answer, because saltless salt cannot be re-salted by any process. echete en heautois hala kai eirēneuete en allēlois — have salt in yourselves and be at peace with one another — the closing application: internal saltness (the quality of covenant-loyalty and preserving fidelity) is the precondition for community peace. The chapter that opened with Transfiguration-glory on the mountain ends with the homely image of salt and the command for peace among the disciples who had been arguing about greatness on the road.

10.18: ti me legeis agathon — why do you call me good? — the question is not a denial of Jesus's goodness but a probe into the rich young man's understanding. Oudeis agathos ei me heis ho theos — no one is good except God alone — if the man means only the common social compliment, the title is misapplied; if he means the absolute goodness that belongs to God alone, then his address to Jesus is more accurate than he knows. The question forces the recognition that the one being addressed either is not good (in which case the title is wrong) or is God (in which case the title barely captures the reality).
10.45: ho huios tou anthropou ouk elthen diakonethenai alla diakonesai kai dounai ten psychen autou lytron anti pollon — the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve and to give his life as a ransom for many — the anti (in exchange for, instead of) is substitutionary: his life is the price paid in the place of the many. Pollon (many) in Semitic usage is inclusive (the many = all, cf. Isa 53:12); it does not limit the scope but emphasizes the number of beneficiaries. The Servant of Isa 53 who gives himself for the many is the Christological background: Jesus names this as his mission-purpose.
