The superlative framing marks this as a unique, unrepeatable historical crisis. Ap' archēs ktiseōs (from the beginning of creation) extends the comparison to the whole of cosmic history.
13.20: ouk an esōthē pasa sarx / dia tous eklektous hous exelexato — no flesh would be saved / for the sake of the elect whom he chose — pasa sarx (all flesh) — the totality of human life. Ekolobōsen (he shortened, cut short) — divine intervention limits the tribulation's duration. The eklektoi (elect, chosen ones) — those chosen by God (hous exelexato, relative clause reinforcing divine agency) — are the reason history does not run to total destruction. Election here functions as a limit on catastrophe.
13.21: idou hōde ho Christos / ekeie — look, here is the Christ / there — the false Messiah-sightings (pointing: hōde = here, ekeie = there) are the deceptive signs that the discourse warned about in v.5–6. The instruction mē pisteuete (do not believe) is absolute — no sign-working Messiah appearing under conditions of tribulation is to be trusted.
13.22: pseudochristoi kai pseudoprophētai / sēmeia kai terata / apoplanān — false Christs and false prophets / signs and wonders / to lead astray — pseudochristos (false-Christ) is a word Mark apparently coins or inherits from early Christian apocalyptic. The coupling with pseudoprophētai (false prophets) mirrors the OT pairing of false prophesy with false kingship. Sēmeia kai terata (signs and wonders) — the credentials of prophetic authority used deceptively. Ei dynaton (if possible) — the conditional acknowledges divine protection of the elect even against miraculous deception.
13.23: hymeis de blepete / proeirēka hymin panta — but you — watch out / I have told you everything beforehand — the emphatic hymeis de (but you) sets the disciples apart from those who will be deceived. Proeirēka (I have foretold, said beforehand) — Jesus's prophylactic disclosure removes any excuse for being caught off guard. The whole discourse is simultaneously prediction and protection.
13.24: meta tēn thlipsin ekeinēn / ho hēlios skotisthēsetai — after that tribulation / the sun will be darkened — the temporal marker meta tēn thlipsin ekeinēn (after that tribulation) introduces a second phase. The celestial imagery — darkened sun, non-shining moon — cites Isaiah 13:10 (oracle against Babylon) and 34:4 (oracle against Edom). In OT prophetic usage, such cosmic convulsions regularly describe the catastrophic fall of kingdoms and the overturning of the old order, not necessarily literal astronomical events.
13.25: hoi asteres esontai ek tou ouranou piptontes — the stars will be falling from heaven — Isaiah 34:4 (kai panta ta astra peseitai). The heavenly powers (hai dynameis hai en tois ouranois saleuthēsontai) being shaken echoes Haggai 2:6 and Hebrews 12:26. The cumulative OT cosmic-disruption imagery functions as the backdrop for the Son of Man's arrival — the old order must be shaken for the new to arrive.
13.27: apostelei tous angelous / episynaxei tous eklektous ek tōn tessarōn anemōn — he will send the angels / and gather the elect from the four winds — apostelei (will send) — the Son of Man dispatches agents of gathering, contrasting with false prophets who scatter. Episynaxei (will gather together) — the prefix epi- intensifies the gathering as comprehensive. Ek tōn tessarōn anemōn (from the four winds) — the four-compass gathering appears in Daniel 7:2 and Isaiah 11:12, evoking the eschatological regathering of dispersed Israel expanded to include all the elect.
13.28: sykē / hē klados autēs hapalos genētai — the fig tree / when its branch becomes tender — the fig tree parable here contrasts with the cursed fig tree of 11:12–14, 20–21. There the fig tree's unfruitfulness symbolized the temple's failure; here the same species serves as a positive timing-signal. When the branch (klados) softens (hapalos, tender, supple) and leaves appear, summer is near — observable, reliable, knowable.
13.29: hotan idēte tauta ginomena / engys estin epi thurais — when you see these things happening / it is near at the door — the observable signs (tauta) indicate nearness. Epi thurais (at the doors) — a threshold image of imminent arrival. The disciples who asked for a sign (v.4) are given a conditional one: the signs within history point to what is near, while the ultimate day remains unknowable (v.32).
13.30: hē genea hautē ou mē parelthē / heos hou tauta panta genētai — this generation will certainly not pass away / until all these things have happened — one of the most debated verses in Mark. Genea normally means the contemporaneous generation (those alive when Jesus speaks). The double negative ou mē parelthē (will certainly not pass away) is emphatic. Most contemporary exegetes read this as referring to the events of 70 CE (the destruction of the temple and tribulation of vv.5–23), with vv.24–27 forming a distinct eschatological horizon. The verse as a whole stakes Jesus's prophetic credibility on near-term verifiability.
13.31: ho de ouranos kai hē gē pareleusontai / hoi de logoi mou ou mē parelthōsin — heaven and earth will pass away / but my words will certainly not pass away — the contrast of ouranos kai gē (heaven and earth — the whole created order) with logoi mou (my words) stakes a remarkable claim. The verbal formula ou mē parelthōsin (will absolutely not pass away) is the same verbal form applied to the generation in v.30. The permanence of Jesus's words exceeds the permanence of the cosmos.
13.32: peri de tēs hēmeras ekeinēs ē tēs hōras oudeis oiden / oude ho huios / ei mē ho patēr — but concerning that day or hour no one knows / not even the Son / but only the Father — this verse is among the most theologically significant in the Gospels. The graduated ignorance — no angel, not even ho huios (the Son) — limits Jesus's knowledge in his incarnate state. The early church found this verse difficult; some patristic writers handled it by distinguishing the Son's divine and human natures. The verse sits within the broader NT framework of kenosis (Phil 2:7). The unknown hēmera kai hōra (day and hour) is precisely what makes watchfulness necessary.
13.33: blepete agrypneite / ouk oidate gar pote ho kairos estin — watch out, stay awake / for you do not know when the time is — agrypneite (stay awake, be vigilant — literally: be sleepless) intensifies blepete. The reason for watchfulness is explicitly the uncertainty of timing: ouk oidate pote (you do not know when). The discourse does not give the disciples a timetable — it gives them an posture.
13.34: hōs anthrōpos apodēmos / thyrōrō / grēgorē — like a man going on a journey / to the doorkeeper / to keep watch — the parable of the absent householder: he distributes responsibility (hekastō to ergon autou, to each his task), with special attention to the doorkeeper. Grēgorē (keep watch, stay awake) — this is the imperative Jesus gives to one specific servant but which the discourse applies to all.
13.35: opsē ē mesonuktiō ē alektorophōnia ē prōi — evening / midnight / cockcrow / early morning — the four Roman night watches. This specific enumeration is not incidental: in the passion narrative that follows, each watch will appear. Gethsemane is late night; Peter's denial occurs at cockcrow (alektorophōnia, 14:68, 72); the early morning watch (prōi) brings Jesus before Pilate (15:1). The Olivet Discourse's watch-schedule is embedded in the passion's temporal structure.
13.36: exaiphnēs elthōn / katheudontas hymas heurē — suddenly coming / finds you sleeping — exaiphnēs (suddenly, unexpectedly — adverb of abrupt appearance) — the opposite of a scheduled, signaled arrival. Katheudontas (sleeping) — the disciples will fail to stay awake in Gethsemane (14:37–41) in a direct dramatic echo of this warning. The irony of their failure is prepared here.
13.37: ho de hymin legō pasin legō / grēgoreite — and what I say to you I say to all / Watch! — the discourse expands its audience beyond the four disciples: pasin (to all). Grēgoreite (Watch! — plural imperative) — the final word of the Olivet Discourse. This imperative will echo tragically in Gethsemane (grēgoreite, 14:34, 38) where the disciples sleep three times while Jesus prays. The climax of the discourse is a one-word command that the disciples will fail within hours.

14.36: abba ho pater, panta dynata soi — Abba, Father, all things are possible for you — Mark preserves the Aramaic Abba (the intimate word for father used within the family, though not exclusively infantile as sometimes claimed; cf. Rom 8:15, Gal 4:6 where it is the Spirit's cry in believers). The address combines intimacy (Abba) with theological acknowledgment (all things are possible for you), making the submission that follows (all the same, not what I will but what you will) the freely chosen embrace of a possible alternative — the Father could have chosen otherwise but Jesus submits to the path chosen.
14.62: ego eimi kai opsesthe ton huion tou anthropou ek dexion kathemenon tes dynameōs kai erchomenon meta ton nephelos tou ouranou — I am; and you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power and coming with the clouds of heaven — Mark's high priest question receives the most direct answer in any Gospel (ego eimi vs. Matthew's you have said so and Luke's more guarded response). The ego eimi (I am) carries the divine-name resonance. The combination of Ps 110:1 (seated at the right hand) and Dan 7:13 (coming with the clouds) is Jesus's Christological self-claim at the moment of maximum vulnerability — arrested, abandoned, tried — which makes it simultaneously the most audacious claim and the most theologically complete self-revelation in Mark.
14.1: to pascha kai ta azyma / meta dyo hēmeras — the Passover and Unleavened Bread / after two days — pascha is the Greek transliteration of Aramaic/Hebrew pesach (Passover), rooted in the verb pasach (to pass over, to spare — Exod 12:13). Azyma (unleavened, without yeast) names the seven-day festival that immediately followed Passover (Lev 23:5–6). Mark's temporal marker meta dyo hēmeras (after two days) places the narrative two days before the Passover meal, creating the Passion Week timeline.
14.2: mē en tē heortē / thorubos — not during the festival / riot/uproar — heortē (festival, feast) — the leaders calculate political timing. Thorubos (uproar, riot, tumult) — their concern is crowd management, not justice. Jerusalem's Passover population swelled from ~50,000 to estimates of 100,000–200,000+ pilgrims, making public order genuinely precarious. The leaders intend stealth; instead Judas provides a private opportunity.
14.3: myrou nardou pistikēs polutelos / kateraxa — nard ointment/perfume of pure quality, very expensive / having broken — nardos is spikenard, an aromatic plant from the Himalayan foothills, imported at great cost. Pistikēs is disputed: most likely from pistos (faithful, trustworthy → genuine/pure); alternatively a trade term. Polutelos (very costly, extremely expensive — superlative register). Kateraxen (having broken — the whole flask poured, not just a portion). Mark names the home as Simōnos tou leprou (Simon the Leper) — the healed condition still defining the person socially.
14.4: aganaktountes / eis ti hē apōleia hautē — being indignant / for what purpose this waste/loss — aganakteō (to be indignant, vexed, offended) — an emotion of moral-social displeasure. Apōleia (waste, destruction, loss) — the same word used for Judas Iscariot (huios tēs apōleias, son of destruction, John 17:12) and for the one who is perishing (2 Thess 2:3). Their economic calculation labels an act of worship as destruction.
14.5: epano dēnariōn triakosiōn / enebrimōnto autē — above 300 denarii / they were rebuking/scolding her — 300 denarii = approximately one year's daily wages for a laborer. Enebrimōnto autē (they were scolding, rebuking her) — the verb embrimaomai conveys strong emotional reproach, almost a snorting of indignation. John 12:4–5 attributes the complaint specifically to Judas Iscariot.
14.6: kalon ergon / egazato eis eme — beautiful/good work / she has done for me — kalon ergon (beautiful work, excellent deed) — kalos combines the beautiful and the good; this is not merely a morally good act but an aesthetically beautiful one, fitting its object. The phrase appears in Matthew 5:16 (let your light shine so they may see your kala erga). Jesus's defense of the woman is emphatic: aphete autēn (leave her alone).
14.7: pantote... eme de ou pantote / eupoiēsai — always... me not always / to do good — the contrast is sharp: the poor (ptōchous) are a permanent fixture of social life and always available for charitable acts (eupoiēsai, to do well/good to). Jesus's physical presence is temporary and unique. The statement is not a dismissal of concern for the poor (Deut 15:11 underlies it) but a corrective about priority and occasion.
14.8: proelaben myrisai to soma mou eis ton entaphiasmon — she has anointed my body beforehand for burial — proelaben (she took beforehand, she anticipated) — the woman's act is retroactively interpreted as prophylactic burial preparation. Entaphiasmos (preparation for burial, burial rites) — Jesus names what will shortly happen to his body and claims this act as its preparation. The anointing that should have happened at the tomb (16:1) happens here at the supper.
14.9: eis to euangelion / eis mnēmosynon autēs — into/with the gospel / for a memorial of her — euangelion (good news/gospel) — wherever the gospel (euangelion, the same word from Mark's opening, 1:1) is proclaimed throughout the world (eis holon ton kosmon), this woman's act will be told. Mnēmosynon (memorial, remembrance) — only here and in Acts 10:4 in the NT. Jesus guarantees the woman's act a memorial that transcends geography and time.
14.10: Ioudas Iskariōth / paradoi auton — Judas Iscariot / to betray him — the juxtaposition of the woman's lavish devotion (vv.3–9) with Judas's defection is stark. Iskariōth: probably from Hebrew ish Qeriyot (man of Kerioth, a Judean town) — making Judas the only non-Galilean of the Twelve. Paradoi (subjunctive of paradidōmi — to hand over, betray) — the verb that has threaded through the passion predictions now moves from announcement to action.
14.11: echarēsan / argyrion / eukairos — they rejoiced / silver money / opportunely — echarēsan (they rejoiced, were glad) — the leaders' emotional response to Judas's offer is joy. Argyrion (silver, silver coins) — Mark does not specify the amount; Matthew 26:15 names 30 pieces of silver (Zech 11:12–13). Eukairos (at the right moment, opportunely) — Judas seeks an occasion (pōs... eukairos auton paradoi): the emphasis is on a private opportunity without the crowd.
14.12: prōtē hēmera tōn azymōn / ethyon to pascha — the first day of the Unleavened Bread / when they slaughtered the Passover — the first day of Unleavened Bread technically began after Passover (Nisan 14), but Josephus and Second Temple usage treated the Passover sacrifice day as the first of the festival complex. Ethyon to pascha (they were sacrificing the Passover lamb) — the temple sacrifices began on Nisan 14 afternoon. The disciples ask where Jesus will eat the pascha (Passover meal), placing the Last Supper in the Passover context.
14.13: keramion hydatos bastazōn — carrying a clay jar of water — water-carrying was typically women's work in first-century Palestine; a man carrying a water jar would be conspicuous. The sign may be a prior arrangement or a supernatural foreknowledge on Jesus's part. Either way, the instruction parallels the triumphal entry preparation (11:2–3) in its confident pre-arrangement.
14.14: katalyma / pascha / phagō — guest room/lodging / Passover / I may eat — katalyma (lodging room, guest room) — the same word used for the inn in Luke 2:7. It refers to a room reserved for guests in a private home, not a commercial inn (pandocheion). Jesus's instruction names himself with the honorific ho didaskalos (the Teacher) — the form a disciple would use to identify his rabbi.
14.15: anagaion mega estrōmenon hetoimon — a large upper room spread/furnished and ready — anagaion (upper room, second-story room) — a hapax legomenon in Mark (also Luke 22:12), referring to the elevated room of a larger house, typically used for special gatherings. Estrōmenon (furnished, spread out — perfect passive of strōnnymi, to spread, to furnish with couches for reclining). The room is already prepared (hetoimon, ready).
14.16: hōs eipen autois — just as he told them — the disciples' discovery that everything was exactly as Jesus predicted (hōs eipen autois) continues Mark's pattern of emphasizing Jesus's authoritative foreknowledge of the Passion events. The precision of fulfilled arrangements is one of the ways Mark underscores divine sovereignty threading through the passion narrative.
14.17: opsias genomenēs / meta tōn dōdeka — evening having come / with the Twelve — opsia (evening) — in Jewish reckoning, the new day began at sunset. Passover (Nisan 15) thus began at sunset on Nisan 14 evening. Jesus's arrival with hoi dōdeka (the Twelve) — the formal number — marks this as the formal Passover gathering. The specification of the Twelve is significant given that one of them will betray and one will deny.
14.18: anakeimenōn / ho esthiōn met' emou — reclining at table / the one eating with me — anakeimai (to recline, to lie back at table) — the reclining posture at meals was Hellenistic-Roman and was adopted at Passover in Second Temple Judaism as a sign of freedom (slaves stood to eat). Ho esthiōn met' emou (the one eating with me) echoes Psalm 41:9 LXX: ho esthiōn artous mou emegalynen ep' eme pternismon (the one eating my bread has lifted his heel against me).
14.19: heis kath' heis / mēti egō — one by one / surely not I — heis kath' heis (one after another, one by one) — a Markan idiom (also 14:35 variant; John 8:9). The pattern of individual self-examination (mēti egō) — the interrogative particle mēti expects a negative answer (literally: am I the one? — expecting the answer no) — reveals the disciples' combination of self-doubt and self-exculpation. Each voices a protestation of innocence that will shortly be tested.
14.20: heis tōn dōdeka / embaptomenos met' emou eis to tryblion — one of the Twelve / the one dipping with me into the bowl — heis tōn dōdeka (one of the Twelve) — the repetition of the Twelve as the frame of betrayal is deliberate: the intimacy of shared table intensifies the treachery. Tryblion (common dish, bowl) — shared dipping into a single bowl was a mark of table-fellowship and trust. The betrayal is embedded in the gesture of shared meal.
14.21: ho huios tou anthrōpou hypagei / kathōs gegraptai / ouai / kalon ēn autō — the Son of Man goes / as it is written / woe / it was good for him — hypagei (goes away, departs — present tense, immediacy) framed by kathōs gegraptai (as it is written — divine scriptural necessity). The tension between divine necessity and human responsibility is at its sharpest here: the betrayal is both ordained and culpable. Kalon ēn autō ei ouk egennēthē ho anthrōpos ekeinos (it were better for that man if he had not been born) — a stark statement about moral accountability that transcends individual psychology.
14.22: eulogēsas / eklasen / edōken / labete, touto estin to soma mou — having blessed/given thanks / broke / gave / take, this is my body — The sequence labon... eulogēsas... eklasen... edōken (having taken... having given thanks/blessed... broke... gave) recurs verbatim in 6:41 (feeding of 5000), establishing a deliberate pattern. Eulogēsas (having blessed) — the berakah (Jewish blessing prayer) over bread. Touto estin to soma mou (this is my body) — the demonstrative pronoun touto (this) without qualification. The Greek estin (is) has generated centuries of debate over what kind of identity-claim is being made.
14.23: eucharistēsas / epion ex autou pantes — having given thanks / they all drank from it — eucharistēsas (having given thanks — from eucharistia, thanksgiving, the root of the English Eucharist). Note the shift from eulogēsas (blessed, for the bread) to eucharistēsas (thanked, for the cup) — both are thanksgiving prayers but with slightly different nuance. Epion ex autou pantes (they all drank from it) — Mark notes universal participation; Matthew 26:27 adds Jesus's explicit command to drink.
14.24: touto estin to haima mou tēs diathēkēs / to ekchhynnomenon hyper pollōn — this is my blood of the covenant / the one being poured out for many — diathēkē (covenant, will/testament) — in the LXX this translates berith (covenant). Mark's text does not include kainēs (new; present in Luke 22:20, 1 Cor 11:25); the best MSS (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) simply read tēs diathēkēs. The phrase echoes Exodus 24:8 (idou to haima tēs diathēkēs — the blood of the covenant, Moses's covenant ratification). Ekchhynnomenon (being poured out — present participle, happening in the very breaking of bread) hyper pollōn (for many — Isa 53:12 echo).
14.25: ouketi ou mē piō / heōs tēs hēmeras ekeinēs / kainon en tē basileia tou theou — I will certainly no more drink / until that day / new in the kingdom of God — the double negative ouketi ou mē is emphatic absolute negation of future drinking. Jesus takes a vow of abstinence from the fruit of the vine until the kingdom arrives. Kainon (new — qualitatively new, not just subsequent) — the eschatological feast will be categorically new. The vow creates anticipatory tension — when will it be fulfilled? The resurrection meals (Luke 24:30–31; John 21:13) begin the answer.
14.26: hymnēsantes / eis to Oros tōn Elaiōn — having sung a hymn / to the Mount of Olives — hymnēsantes (having sung a hymn) — at Passover, the Hallel (Psalms 113–118) was divided: Psalms 113–114 sung before the meal, Psalms 115–118 after. The group's final hymn likely included Psalm 118 — which Jesus had cited twice in the past days (11:9–10; 12:10–11). The Mount of Olives (Oros tōn Elaiōn) was where Zechariah 14:4 expected divine intervention; it is also where David fled weeping from Absalom (2 Sam 15:30).
14.27: skandalisthēsesthe / pataxō ton poimena / ta probata diaskorpisthēsontai — you will all be made to stumble / I will strike the shepherd / the sheep will be scattered — skandalisthēsesthe (you will be caused to stumble/fall away) from skandalizomai — to be tripped, to become an occasion of offense or apostasy. Zechariah 13:7 citation: pataxate ton poimena kai diaskorpisthēsontai ta probata (strike the shepherd and the sheep will be scattered). Jesus applies the divine judgment of Zechariah's striking to his own arrest — he is the shepherd being struck.
14.28: meta to egerthēnai me / proaxō hymas eis tēn Galilaian — after my being raised / I will go before you to Galilee — the passive egerthēnai (being raised — divine passive) is woven into the announcement of the scattering: resurrection and regathering are already foretold in the prediction of abandonment. Proaxō (I will go before/ahead of you) — the same verb used for the colt untying (11:2 variant) and the disciples going ahead. Galilee, where the disciples were called, will be where they are re-commissioned.
14.29: ei kai pantes skandalisthēsontai, all' ouk egō — even if all are caused to stumble, yet not I — Peter's protestation uses the strongest concessive structure available: ei kai (even if) acknowledges the possibility of universal failure while exempting himself. The irony is complete: Peter's self-confidence is the setup for the most detailed denial account in the Gospel. His boast is immediately countered by Jesus's specific prediction.
14.30: dis alektora phōnēsai / treis aparnēsē me — before a cock crows twice / you will disown me three times — Mark uniquely specifies dis (twice) — the cock crows twice in Mark's account (v.68, 72). The other Gospels mention only one crow. Aparnēsē (you will deny/disown — future indicative, not subjunctive — certainty, not possibility). Sēmeron (today) and en tē nukti tautē (in this night) — the denial is immediate and specific.
14.31: ek perissou / syn soi apothanein / ou mē aparnēsomai / pantes de hōsautōs elegon — vehemently/exceedingly / to die with you / I will absolutely not disown you / and all likewise were saying — ek perissou (out of excess, exceedingly, emphatically) intensifies Peter's vehemence. Ou mē aparnēsomai (I will absolutely not disown) — double negative, the strongest denial of future denial. Pantes de hōsautōs elegon (and all were likewise saying — imperfect, ongoing declarations) — the plural failure is prepared alongside the individual failure.
14.32: Gethsēmani / kathisate hōde heōs proseuxōmai — Gethsemane / sit here while I pray — Gethsēmani is Greek transliteration of Aramaic gat shemane (oil press) — an olive grove on the lower slopes of the Mount of Olives where olive oil was extracted. The irony of the name is rich: Jesus will be pressed here as olives are pressed. The instruction to the disciples (kathisate hōde, sit here) separates them from the inner three, then Jesus separates further from the three.
14.33: ekthambein / adēmonein — to be greatly alarmed/astonished / to be in great distress/troubled — ekthambein (to be greatly amazed, alarmed — a compound of thambos, astonishment/terror) and adēmonein (to be in anguish, heavily distressed, at a loss — the etymology uncertain; possibly related to being away from home, out of one's element). These are among the strongest Greek terms for emotional-psychological distress available. Mark's account of Gethsemane is more raw than the parallel accounts.
14.34: perilypos estin hē psychē mou heōs thanatou — my soul is deeply grieved to death/unto death — perilypos (surrounded by grief, overwhelmed with sorrow — the peri- prefix indicates comprehensive surrounding). Psalm 42:5–6 LXX: hina ti perilypos ei psychē mou (why are you cast down, O my soul) — but Jesus's version is not a rhetorical lament-question but a declaration of actual state. Heōs thanatou (unto death) — grief at the limit of endurable human experience; cf. Jonah 4:9. The command to grēgoreite (keep watch) echoes the Olivet Discourse (13:37).
14.35: proselthōn mikron / epipten epi tēs gēs / hina... parelthē hē hōra — going forward a little / fell on the earth / that the hour might pass — epipten epi tēs gēs (fell upon the ground) — the prostration posture of prayer, expressing complete submission. Hōra (hour) — the Passion's appointed time, already marked as coming (2:19–20; 10:38; 13:32; 14:41). The prayer in the imperfect (prosēucheto, he was praying — iterative, ongoing prayer): his request is that if possible the hour might pass.
14.37: katheudontas / Simōn, katheudeis / ouk ischysas — sleeping / Simon, are you sleeping? / were you not able — Jesus addresses Peter by his old name Simōn, not Peter — a moment of disappointment that reverts to the pre-calling name. Ouk ischysas mian hōran grēgorēsai (were you not strong enough to watch one hour) — the irony of the one who just swore to die with Jesus unable to remain awake for one hour. Grēgorēsai (to stay awake/watch) — the Gethsemane failure is the Olivet Discourse failure made concrete.
14.38: grēgoreite kai proseuchesthe / to pneuma prothymon, hē de sarx asthenēs — watch and pray / the spirit willing, the flesh weak — the pairing of grēgoreite (watch, be awake) and proseuchesthe (pray) — together the disciples' active responsibility for remaining in the posture of readiness. To pneuma prothymon (the spirit is eager/willing, ready) — prothymos (eager, willing, forward-leaning). Hē sarx asthenēs (the flesh is weak) — not a dualistic condemnation of the body but an acknowledgment of creaturely limitation. The saying applies to Jesus himself as he prays against the flesh-limitation.
14.39: palin apelthōn proseuxato ton auton logon eipōn — going away again he prayed saying the same word — the repetition of prayer using ton auton logon (the same word/saying) indicates the singular content of Gethsemane's prayer: the cup, the Father's will. Three times Jesus returns to the same petition (vv.35–36, 39, 41). The pattern of threefold repetition mirrors Peter's coming threefold denial — both structures centered on the same night.
14.40: bebarēmenoi / ouk ēdeisan ti apokrithōsin autō — weighed down / they did not know what to answer him — bebarēmenoi (weighed down, heavy — perfect passive participle of bareō, to burden, to make heavy; used of sleep-heaviness also in Luke 9:32 at the transfiguration). Their inability to answer (ouk ēdeisan ti apokrithōsin) stands in painful contrast to Peter's earlier verbose confidence. Speechlessness in the face of Jesus's grief.
14.41: apechei / hē hōra ēlthen / paradidotai ho huios tou anthrōpou — it is enough / the hour has come / the Son of Man is betrayed — apechei is one of the most debated words in Mark. It can mean: (1) it is enough/sufficient; (2) he/it is far/distant; (3) the debt is paid (a papyrus technical term for receipt of payment). Context and syntax are both ambiguous. Most translations follow option 1. Hē hōra ēlthen (the hour has come — aorist, it arrived) — the appointed time has arrived. Paradidotai (is being betrayed — present passive, ongoing action happening right now).
14.42: egeiresthe agōmen / idou ho paradidous me — rise, let us go / look, the one betraying me — egeiresthe (rise! — imperative) echoes the resurrection language that Mark uses for Jesus's own rising (ēgerthē). Agōmen (let us go — hortatory subjunctive) — not flight but advance toward what comes. Engiken (has drawn near) — the betrayer's approach uses the same verb as the kingdom announcement (1:15: ēngiken hē basileia tou theou, the kingdom has drawn near). The hour that has drawn near is the kingdom come in judgment and redemption.
14.43: heis tōn dōdeka / meta machairōn kai xylōn / para tōn archiereōn — one of the Twelve / with swords and clubs / from the chief priests — the identification of Judas as heis tōn dōdeka (one of the Twelve) is repeated from v.10 and v.20 — the emphasis on insider betrayal. The crowd's weapons (machairai, swords; xyla, wooden clubs or staves) represent official force from the temple establishment. The night arrest is consistent with the leaders' plan to avoid public confrontation.
14.44: syssēmon / kataphilēsō / asphalizom kai apagete — agreed signal / I will kiss — hold him / seize and lead away safely — syssēmon (agreed signal, pre-arranged sign — a hapax legomenon in NT). The kiss (phileō) was a normal greeting between teacher and disciple; using it as a signal of betrayal inverts intimacy into treachery. Kataphilēsō (I will kiss — the compound kata- intensifies: to kiss warmly, affectionately, effusively). The betrayal with an intensified kiss is the ultimate irony of table-fellowship turned to treachery.
14.45: Rhabbi / katephilēsen auton — Rabbi / kissed him — Mark uses the Aramaic title Rhabbi (my great one, teacher) — the disciple's form of address for a respected teacher. The verb katephilēsen (aorist of kataphileō) carries out the pre-arranged act. The stark brevity of Mark's account — no extended dialogue as in Matthew — makes the kiss feel more abrupt and cold.
14.46: epebalan tas cheiras autō kai ekratēsan auton — they laid their hands on him and seized him — epiballō tas cheiras (to lay hands on, to seize — the standard idiom for arrest by authority). Ekratēsan (they seized/held firmly — from krateō, to be strong, to hold). The same vocabulary was used for the leaders' desire to arrest Jesus (12:12; 14:1) — now their intent is enacted.
14.47: tis tōn parestēkotōn / apekopsen autou to ōtarion — one of those standing by / cut off his ear — tis (a certain one, unnamed — Mark leaves the swordsman anonymous; John 18:10 names him as Peter). Ōtarion is a diminutive of ous (ear), meaning ‘little ear’ — possibly a slice of the outer ear rather than the entire ear. The act is defensive, impulsive, and immediately counterproductive — Jesus will use it as a teaching moment in Luke 22:51 (healing the ear).
14.48: hōs epi lēstēn / meta machairōn kai xylōn — as against a bandit/robber / with swords and clubs — lēstēs (robber, bandit, brigand) — also the word used for those crucified alongside Jesus (15:27). Jesus's question exposes the absurdity: armed force against one who taught publicly in the temple daily. The question implicitly asks: if I were dangerous, why not arrest me openly? The answer is the leaders' fear of the crowd (v.2).
14.49: kath' hēmeran ēmēn pros hymas... hai graphai plērōthōsin — daily I was with you... the scriptures must be fulfilled — kath' hēmeran (day by day, daily — the public, transparent nature of Jesus's teaching). Plērōthōsin hai graphai (that the scriptures might be fulfilled) — the passive subjunctive expresses divine necessity through scriptural fulfillment. This is the interpretive key Mark places over the entire arrest scene: it is not accident but fulfillment.
14.50: pantes aphentes auton ephygon — having left him all fled — five Greek words containing one of the most devastating statements in the Gospels. Pantes (all) — the universality is stark after their universal protestation of loyalty (v.31). Aphentes (having abandoned, left behind — aorist participle of aphiēmi; the same verb for Peter's abandonment of his nets at the call). The fulfillment of Zechariah 13:7 (the scattered sheep, v.27) is immediate and total.
14.51: neaniskos tis / syndōn / periballomenos epi gymnou — a certain young man / a linen cloth / having thrown it around his naked body — neaniskos (young man) — Mark's second use; the first is the rich young ruler (10:17 variant); the third will be the angel in the tomb (16:5). Syndōn (linen cloth, fine linen — the same word for Jesus's burial shroud, 15:46) wrapped around a naked (gymnos) body. The identity of this young man has generated sustained scholarly debate: some identify him as Mark himself; others as a baptismal or resurrection-symbolism figure.
14.52: apheis tēn sindona gymnos ephygen — leaving behind the linen cloth he fled naked — the young man's flight recapitulates the disciples' flight in miniature — and heightens it: he loses even his covering. Gymnos (naked) — the most shameful state in ancient Mediterranean culture. The episode has no immediate narrative function except to record an embarrassing/curious detail that eyewitness memory would preserve. The linen cloth he leaves behind will appear again as Jesus's burial cloth (15:46).
14.53: pros ton archierea / synerchontai pantes hoi archiereis — to the high priest / all the chief priests came together — archiereus (high priest) — Caiaphas, appointed by the Romans and serving ca. 18–36 CE. The assembly of archiereis kai hoi presbyteroi kai hoi grammateis (chief priests and elders and scribes) represents the full Sanhedrin — the seventy-one member Jewish supreme council. Night sessions of the Sanhedrin were technically irregular; the procedural irregularities have been debated extensively.
14.54: apo makrothen ēkolouthei / sunkatēmenos meta tōn hypēretōn — at a distance he was following / sitting with the guards — apo makrothen (from far off) — Peter's following is marked by distance; the word creates spatial and moral separation. He gets as far as the courtyard but no further. Sunkatēmenos meta tōn hypēretōn (sitting with the servants/guards) — Peter is physically positioned among Jesus's adversaries while Jesus is being tried. The scene cuts between the two simultaneously, as Mark will do through vv.55–72.
14.55: to synedrion / martyrian kata tou Iēsou / eis to thanatōsai auton — the Sanhedrin / testimony against Jesus / to put him to death — synedrion (the sitting-together council — the Sanhedrin). The council is described as ezetoun (imperfect — were seeking, kept seeking) testimony against Jesus; the outcome is already determined, and the process is finding legal cover for a predetermined verdict. Martyrian (testimony, witness) — the legal form is maintained even as the purpose is execution.
14.56: pseudomarturoun / isai ouk ēsan — they were testifying falsely / they were not equal/consistent — pseudomarturein (to testify falsely, to give false witness — violates the ninth commandment, Exod 20:16). The legal requirement for capital cases in Jewish law was two or three consistent witnesses (Deut 17:6; 19:15). Isai ouk ēsan (they were not equal/consistent) — the testimonies did not agree with each other, making them inadmissible.
14.57: anastantes tines — certain ones standing up — the specific phrase anastantes tines (certain ones standing up) introduces the witness-group that will provide the one charge that requires a direct response from Jesus. Their testimony concerns the temple, the central institution of Jewish identity.
14.58: ton naon touton ton cheiropoiēton / acheiropoiēton — this temple made with hands / not made with hands — cheiropoiētos (made-with-hands — a compound adjective) and acheiropoiētos (not-made-with-hands) are theologically loaded terms in the LXX (Isa 2:18; 10:11; 16:12; 19:1) where hand-made gods are idols. A naon acheiropoiēton (sanctuary not-made-with-hands) implies a transcendent, heavenly temple. The witnesses distort Jesus's saying (cf. John 2:19), but the underlying concept — that Jesus would replace the temple with a community or body — is not entirely unfounded.
14.59: oude houtōs isē ēn hē martyria autōn — not even so was their testimony equal/consistent — the double failure of the witnesses (v.56 general inconsistency; v.59 the specific temple charge also inconsistent) creates a legal impasse. The Sanhedrin's case cannot proceed to execution on available testimony. The high priest therefore takes the unprecedented step of placing Jesus under direct oath.
14.60: anastas ho archiereus / oud' apokriē ouden — the high priest standing up / you answer nothing? — the high priest's rising from his seat is a formal escalation — he takes personal charge of the interrogation. His double question (ti houtoi sou katamarturousin, what do these testify against you?) invites self-incrimination. Jesus's continued silence (esigōpan, v.61) fulfills the servant of Isaiah 53:7 who was silent before his shearers.
14.61: esigōpan kai ouden apekrinatō / ho christos ho huios tou eulogētou — was silent and answered nothing / the Christ, the Son of the Blessed One — esigōpan (was silent — imperfect, maintained silence throughout). Isaiah 53:7 LXX: ouk anoigei to stoma autou (he does not open his mouth). Eulogētos (Blessed One) — a circumlocution for God, common in rabbinic usage to avoid the divine name. The high priest's question directly invokes the Messianic title: ho Christos (the Anointed One) and ho huios tou eulogētou (the Son of the Blessed One) — both drawn from the highest claims Jesus's followers made about him.
14.63: diarrēxas tous chitōnas autou — tearing his tunics — the high priest's tearing of his garments was a prescribed response to blasphemy in Jewish law (m. Sanh. 7:5): hearing a capital blasphemy, the judge tears his garments and cannot re-stitch them. The plural chitōnas (inner garments, tunics) indicates the formal gesture. The question that follows (ti eti chreian echomen martyrōn, why do we still have need of witnesses?) signals that Jesus's own words have provided the charge.
14.64: blasphēmias / pantes katekrinan auton enochos thanatou einai — blasphemy / they all condemned him as liable to death — blasphēmia (blasphemy — speaking words that dishonor or usurp the divine) — in Jewish law, capital blasphemy involved pronouncing the divine Name (m. Sanh. 7:5). Jesus's egō eimi combined with the Psalm 110 and Daniel 7 imagery was heard as a divine claim. Pantes katekrinan (they all condemned) — the universal condemnation by the council.
14.65: emptein autō / perikalyptein to prosōpon autou / kolaphizein auton / prophēteuson — to spit on him / to blindfold his face / to strike him with the fist / Prophesy! — emptuō (to spit on — the supreme gesture of contempt in Mediterranean culture; Num 12:14; Isa 50:6 LXX: to de prosōpon mou ouk apestrepsamen apo aischynēs emptysmatōn). Kolaphizein (to strike with the fist, to box). The prophēteuson (Prophesy! — command) is ironic: as they mock the prophet with blindfolded blows, asking who struck him, he is simultaneously on trial for truthful prophecy about his death and resurrection.
14.66: katō en tē aulē / paidiskōn tou archiereōs — below in the courtyard / of the servant girls of the high priest — the spatial marker katō (below, in the courtyard) contrasts with Jesus's position above in the inner chamber. Paidiskē (servant girl, female slave) — one of the lowest-status people in the household. Peter's faith is exposed by the lowest-ranking member of the high priest's household, not by a person of authority.
14.67: idousa ton Petron / kai embleplousa autō — seeing Peter / and looking closely at him — emblepsasa (looking closely, peering intently — em- prefix intensifies the gaze). The servant girl's scrutiny is deliberate: she recognizes him. Kai su meta tou Nazarēnou Iēsou ēstha (you also were with the Nazarene, Jesus) — identifying Jesus by his Galilean town of origin (Nazarēnos).
14.68: ērnēsato / oute oida oute epistamai ti sy legeis / proaulion — he denied / I neither know nor understand what you are saying / the forecourt — ērnēsato (he denied — aorist of arneomai, to deny, disown) — the first denial. The form of denial (oute oida oute epistamai, I neither know nor understand) uses two different Greek verbs for knowing, emphasizing the completeness of professed ignorance. Proaulion (the forecourt, gateway vestibule — a hapax in the NT), the threshold of the outer court, signals Peter's physical retreat even as he lingers.
14.69: hē paidiskē idousa auton ērxato palin legein — the servant girl seeing him began again to say — the same servant girl (or possibly another, but Mark's hē paidiskē suggests the same) resumes the identification. The palin (again) marks the second encounter. Peter's presence in the forecourt has only delayed, not ended, his exposure.
14.70: Galilaios ei / hē lalia sou homoiazei — you are a Galilean / your speech is like them — those standing near identify Peter by his accent: hē lalia sou homoiazei (your manner of speech resembles them) — Galilean Aramaic had a distinctive pronunciation, particularly collapsing of guttural distinctions (Matthew 26:73 adds dēlos gar ei, for you are obvious). Regional dialect functions as unintended identity marker.
14.71: anathematizein / omnynai / ouk oida ton anthrōpon touton — to curse / to swear / I do not know this man — anathematizein (to put under a curse, to invoke a curse upon oneself) — the strongest available oath form; to call a curse upon yourself if what you say is false. Combined with omnynai (to swear an oath), this is the most emphatic denial possible. The phrase ton anthrōpon touton (this man) — the impersonal, distancing reference stands in stark contrast to Peter's earlier declarations of willingness to die with him.
14.72: ek deuterou / anemnēsthē / epibas eklaien — for the second time / he remembered / going out he wept — ek deuterou (for the second time — Mark's unique detail of the second crow). Anemnēsthē (he remembered — aorist of anamimnēskomai, to remember, to call to mind). The remembering is triggered by the second crow — memory and physical cue aligned. Epibas eklaien — epibas is one of the most difficult words in Mark: possibly ‘throwing himself down’ (from epiballō), or ‘beginning’ (from epiballō in the sense of setting to), or ‘going outside’ (from epibainō). Whatever the precise rendering, the result is: eklaien (he wept — imperfect, was weeping, kept weeping). The chapter ends with the sound of Peter's tears.

15.34: eloi eloi lema sabachthani — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me — Mark uses a slightly different Aramaic form from Matthew; both transliterate Ps 22:1. The double divine address (my God, my God) is the Psalm's opening cry — urgent, trusting, and anguished simultaneously. Mark's stark account of the crucifixion (no earthquake, no opened tombs as in Matthew, no thief-repentance as in Luke) makes the cry of dereliction the climactic statement: the Son of God dies abandoned by God, bearing what sinners deserve, before the vindication that Ps 22's own ending provides (vv.24-31: YHWH has not despised the affliction of the afflicted one).
15.39: alethos houtos ho anthropos huios en theou — truly this man was Son of God — the centurion's confession is the human climax of Mark's Christology: the one who was declared Son of God by the Father at the baptism (1:11) is recognized as Son of God by a Roman soldier at the death. The inclusio is complete. Mark has no birth narrative because he begins with the Son's public identification (baptism-declaration) and ends with the soldier's confession — the entire Gospel is bounded by the Son of God identity, revealed through the Servant's death rather than despite it.
15.1: prōi / symboylion hetoimasantes / dēsantes / Pilatō paredōkan — very early / having made a plan / having bound / handed over to Pilate — prōi (very early morning — the fourth watch, before dawn). Symboylion hetoimasantes (having made/prepared a plan) — the formal council decision for delivery to Roman authority. Dēsantes (having bound Jesus) — the binding that continues from the arrest (14:46). Paredōkan (handed over — aorist of paradidōmi) — the chain of betrayal reaches Pilate: from Judas (14:10) to the priests (14:53) to Pilate. Hēgemōni (governor — the Roman title for the prefect of Judea).
15.2: sy ei ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn / sy legeis — are you the king of the Jews? / you say it — basileus tōn Ioudaiōn (King of the Jews) — the Roman political charge, appearing five times in Mark 15 (vv.2, 9, 12, 18, 26). Sy legeis (you say so / that is your word) — an affirmation but not a straightforward yes. The construction is the same as in 14:62 where Jesus replied to a different question with egō eimi. Here the response is more distancing: the title is Pilate's framing, not Jesus's preferred category. Some translators render it as acknowledgment; others as subtle qualification.
15.3: katēgoroun / polla — were accusing / many things — the imperfect katēgoroun (were accusing, kept accusing — continuous action) pictures sustained pressure from the chief priests. Polla (many things) without specification — the plural accusations are left vague; what matters narratively is that Jesus does not respond to any of them (v.5). The charges were presumably expanded from the Sanhedrin proceedings (14:55–64) into Roman political language.
15.4: oudeno apokriē / posa sou katamarturousin — you answer nothing / how many things they testify against you — Pilate's astonishment at Jesus's silence is introduced with his second direct question. The cumulative weight of multiple accusers pressing charges while the accused stands mute is extraordinary. Isaiah 53:7 (the silent servant) provides the scriptural frame, though Mark does not cite it explicitly here.
15.5: ouketi ouden apekrinatō / thaumazein — he answered nothing more / to be amazed — ouketi ouden (no longer anything — emphatic double negation). Thaumazein ton Pilaton (Pilate to be amazed) — thaumazō is Mark's word for Jesus's own astonishment at unbelief (6:6) and for the crowds' amazement at his teaching. Here Pilate the pagan administrator is amazed at Jesus's self-mastery. The reversal is pointed: the one with Roman authority is confounded by the prisoner.
15.6: apolyen auton hena desmon / paretounto — he would release one prisoner / they would request — apolyen (imperfect — he used to release, it was his custom); the construction describes the Passover amnesty as a repeating practice. Desmon (prisoner, bound one). The historicity of this custom has been debated — it is not attested in Roman or Jewish sources outside the Gospels — but may represent a local accommodation to Jewish festival expectations.
15.7: Barabbas / stasiastais / phonon pepoiēkei en tē stasei — Barabbas / insurrectionists / had committed murder in the insurrection — Barabbas is Aramaic bar Abbas (son of Abbas or son of the father — abba). The naming choice creates a profound irony: the crowd releases a murderous bar Abbas (son of the father) and demands the death of the one who truly is the Son of the Father. Stasiastai (rebels, insurrectionists) and phonos (murder) — Barabbas is the genuine political criminal that Rome accused Jesus of being.
15.8: anaboēsas / parekalei — crying out / began to ask — anaboēsas (crying out, going up crying — the ana- prefix may indicate the crowd surging forward). The imperfect parekalei (were asking) describes the crowd petitioning Pilate to honor the release custom. The crowd's demand is distinct from the chief priests' manipulation (v.11) — they initially only want the customary release, not specifically the death of Jesus.
15.9: thelete apolyso hymin ton basilea tōn Ioudaiōn — do you want me to release to you the King of the Jews? — Pilate offers Jesus using the title the priests used in the charge. The question is politically loaded: accepting the release implicitly endorses the Messianic claim the priests are trying to suppress. The crowd's eventual rejection of this offer is engineered by the priests (v.11), not spontaneous.
15.10: eginōsken hoti dia phthonon — he knew that out of envy — phthonos (envy, jealous resentment — one of the vices listed in Paul's catalogs, e.g. Rom 1:29; Gal 5:21; the word for the malicious begrudging of another's status or success). Pilate reads the priests' motive correctly: Jesus's popularity threatens their institutional power. This is the narrator's direct attribution of motive, a rare moment of interiority given to the Roman governor.
15.11: aneseisan ton ochlon — stirred up the crowd — anaseiō (to shake up, agitate, stir — a vivid verb for rabble-rousing; ana- = upward motion + seiō = to shake). The chief priests actively work the crowd to redirect the Passover amnesty demand toward Barabbas. The manipulation is openly narrated: the crowd's eventual cry is orchestrated, not organic.
15.12: ti oun poiēsō... ton basilea tōn Ioudaiōn — what then shall I do with the King of the Jews? — Pilate's question uses the royal title again, perhaps deliberately provoking the crowd. The three-way political trap: Pilate cannot release a claimed king without appearing to endorse sedition; he cannot condemn an innocent man without injustice; he cannot satisfy the priests without becoming their instrument. His solution — letting the crowd decide — abdicates judicial responsibility.
15.13: staurosōn / stauron — crucify! / cross — stauroō (to crucify) from stauros (stake, post, cross). Crucifixion was designed for maximum suffering and public deterrence — reserved for slaves, non-Roman criminals, and political rebels. The naked victim was fixed (usually nailed) to the cross and died from a combination of exposure, dehydration, and asphyxiation. The crowd's demand requires no legal justification in their answer — just the demand.
15.14: ti gar epoiēsen kakon / perissōs ekraxan — what evil has he done? / all the more loudly they shouted — Pilate's question (ti kakon epoiēsen, what evil/harm did he do?) is the closest thing to an acquittal in the Markan trial narrative. He finds no basis for the charge but lacks the moral authority to act on this finding. Perissōs (exceedingly, all the more — an intensifier from perisseuo, to exceed, to abound).
15.15: to hikanon poiēsai / phragellōsas / paredōken — to satisfy / having flogged / handed over — to hikanon poiēsai (to do enough, to satisfy — a Latinism: satisfacere rendered in Greek) — legal terminology for satisfying a legal demand. Phragellōsas (having flogged — from Latin flagellum, the Roman military scourging whip; leather thongs embedded with bone and metal fragments that could lacerate flesh to the bone). The flogging preceded the crucifixion and often killed the condemned before they reached the cross. Paredōken (handed over) — the final link in the paradidōmi chain.
15.16: Praitōrion / holēn tēn speiran — the Praetorium / the whole cohort — Praitōrion (Latin praetorium) — the governor's official residence and headquarters in Jerusalem, either the Antonia Fortress adjacent to the temple or Herod's palace. Speira (cohort — a Roman military unit of approximately 480-600 soldiers; one tenth of a legion). The phrase holēn tēn speiran (the whole cohort) may be hyperbolic, suggesting a large mocking assembly.
15.17: porphyran / stephanos ex akanthōn — purple (robe) / crown of thorns — porphyra (purple cloth — the color of imperial and royal robes in Rome; Tyrian purple dye was extraordinarily expensive). The soldiers mock the Messianic claim with the visible symbols of kingship: robe and crown. Akanthōn (thorns) — the crown may have used Ziziphus spina-christi (Christ-thorn) or Poterium spinosum, common thorny plants of Palestine. The mock coronation inverts every element of royal investiture.
15.18: chaire, basileu tōn Ioudaiōn — Hail, King of the Jews! — chaire (Hail! / Greetings! — the standard Greek salutation) parodies the Roman imperial acclamation Ave Caesar (Hail Caesar). The soldiers perform a satirical coronation ceremony, complete with the royal greeting, the purple robe, and the crown. In performing the mockery, they unknowingly enact the truth of the title they ridicule.
15.19: ekalamō etypton autou tēn kephalēn / eneptyon autō / gonypetountes prosekunoun autō — they beat his head with a reed / spat on him / falling on knees worshiped him — The triad of humiliation: striking with the mock scepter (the reed), spitting (the gesture of ultimate contempt), and parodic worship (proskuneō — the genuine word for kneeling adoration). The soldiers complete the scene of Isaiah 53:3 (despised and rejected) while performing the gestures that are theologically due to the one they are mocking.
15.20: enepaixan / exagousin auton hina staurōsōsin auton — they mocked / they lead him out to crucify him — empazō (to mock, play with, treat as a toy) — the mockery complete; now the execution. The shift from imperfect to present tense (exagousin, they lead out — vivid historical present) accelerates the narrative pace. The victim was typically made to carry the horizontal beam (patibulum) of his cross to the execution site.
15.21: Kyrēnaion / Alexandrou kai Rhouphoy patera / agorogeuousin — a Cyrenian / father of Alexander and Rufus / they press into service — Kyrēnaios (from Cyrene — a North African city with a large Jewish diaspora community). Mark identifies Simon by his sons: Alexandrou kai Rhouphoy patera (father of Alexander and Rufus) — this parenthetical identifies Simon to a community that knew his sons. Romans 16:13 mentions a Rufus in Rome, possibly the same person. Agoregeuousin (they press into compulsory service — a military requisition term, from Latin angaria).
15.22: Golgothon / Kranion Topos — Golgotha / Place of the Skull — Golgothon is Greek transliteration of Aramaic Gulgolta (skull). Mark translates for his audience: Kranion Topos (Place of Skull — kranion is the Greek anatomical term for skull, root of the English cranium). The name likely described the rocky outcrop's shape. The site was apparently a public execution location outside the city walls (Heb 13:12) on a main road, maximizing visibility as deterrence.
15.23: esmyrnismenon oinon / ouk elaben — wine mixed with myrrh / he did not take it — smyrna (myrrh — a bitter resinous gum from the Commiphora tree, used medicinally and as an analgesic). Wine with myrrh was offered as a mild painkiller or narcotic before crucifixion. Jesus's refusal (ouk elaben, he did not take) is deliberate — he chooses to face the cross fully conscious. The refusal is consistent with the Gethsemane prayer's eventual submission: he will drink the cup his Father gives him (14:36).
15.24: staurousin auton / diamerizontai ta himatia / ballon klēron — they crucified him / they divide his garments / casting lots — Mark records the crucifixion itself in three words: kai staurousin auton (and they crucified him) — no elaboration of the physical details. The narrative moves immediately to the division of garments, a Psalm 22:18 echo: diemerisanto ta himatia mou heautois kai epi ton himatismon mou ebalon klēron (they divided my garments among themselves and cast lots for my clothing). The Scripture is enacted without being cited.
15.25: hōra tritē / estaurōsan auton — the third hour / they crucified him — the third hour in Jewish/Roman daytime reckoning is approximately 9 AM (counting from sunrise). This differs from John 19:14, which places the trial still underway at the sixth hour (noon). The discrepancy has generated extensive discussion; most scholars treat it as a difference in timekeeping conventions or in the evangelist's rhetorical purposes. Mark's third-hour crucifixion allows for three hours of darkness (noon to 3 PM, vv.33–37).
15.26: epigraphē tēs aitias / HO BASILEUS TŌN IOUDAIŌN — the written notice of the charge / THE KING OF THE JEWS — epigraphē (inscription, written notice) — the titulus (Latin): a placard specifying the condemned's crime, typically carried before him in procession and then fixed above the cross. The charge is not blasphemy (the Sanhedrin's verdict) but sedition — claiming royal authority. In Roman law, this was the capital crime. The irony: the charge is the truth written above his head.
15.27: lēstas / ek dexiōn / ex euōnymōn — bandits/rebels / on his right / on his left — lēstai (bandits, rebels — the same word Jesus used at his arrest: as against a lēstēn, 14:48). The placement between two rebels echoes Isaiah 53:12 (meta anomōn elogisthē, he was numbered with the transgressors) — a connection made explicit in v.28 (the textual note below). The positioning ek dexiōn / ex euōnymōn (right / left) ironically fulfills the disciples' request for the seats of honor at his right and left (10:37, 40).
15.28: Textual note: Verse 28 is absent from the earliest and most important manuscripts — Sinaiticus (01), Vaticanus (03), Alexandrinus (02), and other significant witnesses. It appears in later manuscripts and reads: kai eplērōthē hē graphē hē legousa, kai meta anomōn elogisthē (and the scripture was fulfilled which says: he was numbered with the transgressors — Isaiah 53:12). Most modern critical editions (NA28, UBS5) place it in a footnote or omit it. It is a correct theological observation about what is happening, but as a verse it is most likely a scribal addition that made explicit what the narrative implies through v.27.
15.29: blasphēmountes / kinountes tas kephalas autōn / ouah — blaspheming / shaking their heads / Aha! — blasphēmountes (speaking injuriously against, blaspheming) — the same verb used for the charge against Jesus (14:64) is now used by the crowd against Jesus. Kinountes tas kephalas autōn (shaking their heads) echoes Psalm 22:7 LXX: pantes hoi theōrountes me exemykterisan me, elalēsan en cheilesin, ekinēsan kephalēn (all who see me mock me; they shoot out the lip, they shake the head). The Passion narrative is saturated with Psalm 22 echoes. Ouah (Aha! — an exclamation of contemptuous triumph).
15.30: sōson seauton / katabasas apo tou staurou — save yourself / coming down from the cross — the taunt inverts the Gospel's central paradox: the one who saves others is unable to save himself precisely because he is saving others. Sōson seauton (save yourself) — the imperative uses the same verb (sōzō) that describes Jesus's healing ministry throughout Mark. The crowds unknowingly express the theological truth: self-saving would have meant non-salvation for others.
15.31: allous esōsen / heauton ou dynatai sōsai — he saved others / he cannot save himself — the chief priests' mockery contains the Gospel in two clauses. Allous esōsen (he saved others — aorist, completed past acts of salvation) — the healings, exorcisms, raising of the dead. Heauton ou dynatai sōsai (he cannot save himself) — the ou dynatai (cannot) uses the same verb-root as dynamos (power) and echoes 10:27 (para anthrōpois adynaton, all' ou para theō, impossible with humans, but not with God). The salvation of others requires the non-salvation of himself.
15.32: ho Christos ho basileus Israēl / katabasatō nyn / idōmen kai pisteusōmen — the Christ, the King of Israel / come down now / that we may see and believe — ho Christos ho basileus Israēl — the scribes combine the Messianic title with the royal designation, formulating the very truth they deny. Idōmen kai pisteusōmen (that we may see and believe) — the conditional structure inverts the Gospel's logic: faith opens to sight, not sight producing faith. Jesus's refusal to perform a sign-miracle here (cf. 8:11–12) is consistent with his entire ministry's logic. Synestaurōmenoi (those crucified with him — even the two rebels join the mockery in Mark).
15.33: skotos egeneto eph' holēn tēn gēn / heōs hōras enatēs — darkness came over the whole land / until the ninth hour — skotos (darkness) from noon (hōras hektēs, sixth hour) until 3 PM (hōras enatēs, ninth hour) — three hours. The darkness echoes Amos 8:9: kai estai en tē hēmera ekeinē, legei kyrios, dysetai ho hēlios mesēmbrias (on that day, says the Lord, the sun will go down at noon). Also Exodus 10:21–23 (the darkness plague over Egypt). The darkness is cosmic participation in the death of the Messiah — or in Mark's register, a sign of divine judgment and mourning.
15.35: Ēlian phōnei — he is calling Elijah — the confusion between Eloi (Aramaic: my God, from El) and Ēlias (Elijah in Greek) is phonetically plausible: both begin with the el- sound. Whether the bystanders genuinely misheard or were mockingly misrepresenting his cry is debated. Elijah was expected to come as a precursor to the end (Mal 4:5; 3:23 LXX); anticipating that Jesus might summon Elijah adds an eschatological layer to the crowd's response.
15.36: oxous / spongon / perititheis kalamo / aphete idōmen — sour wine / sponge / placing on a reed / let us see — oxos (sour wine, vinegar — cheap wine for soldiers and laborers; different from the myrrh-wine offered in v.23). Psalm 69:21 LXX: edōkan eis to brōma mou cholēn kai eis tēn dipsan mou epotisan me oxos (they gave me gall for my food and vinegar for my thirst). The narrator's comment aphete idōmen ei erchetai Ēlias (let us see if Elijah comes) — another instance of the crowds expecting a miraculous rescue that does not come.
15.37: apheis phōnēn megalēn exepneusen — letting out a loud voice he breathed his last — exepneusen (he breathed out, expired — from ekpneō: ek- = out + pneō = to breathe; related to pneuma, spirit/breath). The last breath is ordinarily labored and quiet in crucifixion (respiratory failure from hanging). A phōnē megalē (loud cry/voice) at the moment of death is physiologically striking and theologically purposive — it is not a death of exhaustion but a deliberate release. John 19:30 makes the deliberateness explicit: paradōken to pneuma (he handed over the spirit).
15.38: to katapetasma tou naou eschisthē eis dyo ap' anōthen heōs katō — the veil of the temple was torn into two from top to bottom — katapetasma (veil, curtain) — either the outer curtain of the sanctuary (visible from the temple court) or the inner veil separating the Holy of Holies (accessible only to the High Priest on Yom Kippur, Lev 16). Eschisthē (was torn — aorist passive of schizō) — the same verb used for the heavens being torn at the baptism (1:10: schizomenous tous ouranous). The tearing of the heavens opened at Jesus's identification as Son; the tearing of the veil accompanies his death. Ap' anōthen heōs katō (from top to bottom — the direction of divine rather than human action).
15.40: apo makrothen / Maria hē Magdalēnē — from far off / Mary the Magdalene — apo makrothen (from a distance) — the women maintain the distance the disciples have also kept (Peter followed apo makrothen, 14:54). Three women are named: Maria hē Magdalēnē (Mary the Magdalene — from Magdala on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee; the Magdalēnē title distinguishes her from the other Marys); Maria hē Iakōbou tou mikrou kai Iōsētos mētēr (Mary the mother of James the small/lesser and of Joseph); Salōmē (named only in Mark among the Synoptics).
15.41: ēkolouthoun autō kai diēkonoun / gynai pollai — were following him and serving him / many women — ēkolouthoun (were following — imperfect, continuous; the discipleship word) and diēkonoun (were serving/ministering — imperfect; the word for table-service and the word Jesus used for his own mission: diakonēsai, 10:45). Mark retroactively identifies these women as disciples from Galilee using the precise vocabulary of discipleship. Gynai pollai (many women) — their number underscores that the male disciples' flight (14:50) was not the whole story of the community's response to the cross.
15.42: paraskeuē / prosabbaton — Preparation Day / pre-Sabbath day — paraskeuē (Preparation — Friday, the day of preparation for the Sabbath) is a technical term found in all four Gospels. Prosabbaton (pre-Sabbath day) is a hapax legomenon in the NT (only here), reinforcing the temporal urgency: burial must occur before sunset when the Sabbath begins (Deut 21:23 requires burial of an executed criminal on the day of death).
15.43: euschēmōn bouleutēs / prosedechomenos tēn basileian tou theou / tolmēsas — a respected council member / awaiting the kingdom of God / daring — euschēmōn (respected, prominent, of good standing — the word for social honor). Bouleutēs (council member — a member of the Sanhedrin, which had just condemned Jesus). Prosdechomenos tēn basileian tou theou (waiting/expecting the kingdom of God) — the phrase identifies Joseph as sympathetic to Jesus's message. Tolmēsas eisēlthen pros ton Pilaton (daring, he went in to Pilate) — tolmaō (to dare, be bold) acknowledges that requesting the body of a crucified rebel was an act of political courage.
15.44: ethaumasen ei ēdē tethnēken / proskaleis ton kentyriōna — was surprised that he was already dead / summoning the centurion — ethaumasen (was surprised, marveled) — crucifixion typically took 24–72 hours; death in approximately 6 hours was fast (Jesus was crucified at ~9 AM and died at ~3 PM). Pilate's inquiry of the centurion constitutes a de facto death certification — a legal verification of death before releasing the body. The centurion's testimony (v.45) provides the official confirmation.
15.45: gnous apo tou kentyriōnos / ptōma / edōrēsato tō Iōsēph — having learned from the centurion / the corpse / he gave it as a gift to Joseph — ptōma (corpse, body of a fallen person — a specific word for a dead body, from piptō, to fall). Edōrēsato (gave as a gift — from dōreomai, to grant, bestow; carries the sense of a favor or gift given from a position of authority). The verb dignifies Joseph's receipt: he is not scavenging but receiving an official grant.
15.46: sindona / katheilon / enetylixen tē sindoni / mnēmeion / lithos — linen cloth / took down / wrapped in the linen cloth / tomb / stone — sindōn (fine linen cloth) — the same word for the young man's garment in 14:51–52; a connection many readers have found significant. Katheilen (took down — technical term for taking down a body from a cross). Enetylixen tē sindoni (wrapped in the linen). The tomb is described as lelatomēmenon ek petras (cut out of rock — a rock-cut tomb); lithos (stone) rolled to the entrance — the stone that will concern the women in 16:3.
15.47: ethēanto / etethē — they observed / was placed — ethēanto (they beheld, observed — from theaomai, to gaze carefully upon; the root of the English theatre). The two Marys (Maria hē Magdalēnē kai Maria hē Iōsētos) are explicitly noted as witnesses of the burial location. Their observation of pou tethetai (where he was placed) is the narrative link to the resurrection discovery: they know where to find the tomb (16:1–2). The women are the continuity witnesses across burial and resurrection.

16.6: egerthe, ouk estin ode — he has been raised; he is not here — the passive egerthe (he was raised, divine passive) identifies the resurrection as God's act, not a resuscitation or self-resurrection. The empty tomb is the negative evidence; the positive evidence is the proclamation (proagei hymas eis ten Galilaian = he is going before you to Galilee). Mark's original ending (assuming 16:8 is the original ending) is characteristically abrupt: the women fled and said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid. The reader is left with the open question that the entire Gospel posed — who do you say that he is? The silence of the women is the silence into which the reader must speak.
16.1: diagenoumenou tou sabbatou / ēgorasan arōmata / aleiposin auton — the Sabbath having passed / bought spices / to anoint him — diagenoumenou (having passed completely — aorist of diaginomai; the Sabbath is over at sunset Saturday). Three women are named: Mary Magdalene, Mary of James, and Salome (consistent with the witnesses at the cross, 15:40). Arōmata (spices, aromatics) for anointing — they had not been able to complete the burial preparation before the Sabbath; the earlier anointing at Bethany (14:8) was anticipatory burial preparation. Aleiposin auton (to anoint him) — the same verb used at Bethany (14:3 cognate).
16.2: lian prōi / tē mia tōn sabbatōn / anateilantos tou hēliou — very early / on the first of the week / the sun having risen — lian prōi (extremely early, deep early morning). Tē mia tōn sabbatōn (on the first of the Sabbaths / first day of the week) — the Aramaic/Jewish counting of days; mia (one/first) refers to Sunday. Anateilantos tou hēliou (the sun having risen — genitive absolute) — the precise temporal marker. The darkness of death (the three hours of darkness, 15:33) is over; the first light of the new day frames the discovery.
16.3: tis apokylisei hēmin ton lithon — who will roll away the stone for us — apokyliō (to roll away — a compound verb; apo- = away + kyliō = to roll). The practical concern as they approach — they have the spices but not the means to open the tomb. The question is never answered: when they arrive, the answer has already been given by an agent greater than any human helper. The stone becomes in Markan theology a marker of divine action.
16.4: anakekylismenonl / megas sphodra — had been rolled back / extremely large — anakekylismenon (had been rolled back — perfect passive of anakylioō; the perfect tense indicates a completed action with present state: it was rolled and remains rolled back). Megas sphodra (very great/large) — the parenthetical note about the stone's size explains why their concern (v.3) was legitimate and why the discovery is unexpected. No human agent is identified as having moved it.
16.5: neaniskon / stolichn leukēn / exethambethesan — a young man / white robe — they were greatly alarmed — neaniskos (young man) — this is the third neaniskos in Mark: the unnamed figure of 14:51–52, the young man of 10:20 (variant), and now this figure at the tomb. The white robe (stolē leukē) distinguishes him from the one in the linen cloth: white garments are angelic vestments (Dan 7:9; Rev 7:9). Exethambethesan (were greatly astonished/alarmed — the strongest compound of thambos used in Mark).
16.7: kai tō Petrō / proagei hymas eis tēn Galilaian / hōs eipen hymin — and to Peter / he is going before you to Galilee / just as he told you — kai tō Petrō (and to Peter) — Peter's specific naming alongside the disciples is remarkable. He had denied Jesus three times (14:66–72); the specific inclusion of his name in the resurrection message is the re-inclusion of the one who most publicly failed. Proagei (is going before — present tense, ongoing action; the risen Lord goes ahead as a shepherd) echoes 14:28 exactly. Hōs eipen hymin (just as he told you) — the fulfillment of the word spoken before the Passion.
16.8: ephugon / tromos kai ekstasis / ouden ouden eipon / ephobounto gar — they fled / trembling and amazement / they said nothing to anyone / for they were afraid — the final verse of the original Gospel (in the best manuscripts). Tromos (trembling — physical trembling of awe and fear) and ekstasis (ecstasy/astonishment — standing outside oneself; the word for trance or profound disorientation before the divine). Ouden ouden eipon (they said nothing to anyone — double ouden, emphatic). The final clause ephobounto gar (for they were afraid) ends with a gar (for) — an unusual and perhaps unprecedented way to end a sentence or a book. Scholars debate whether this was Mark's intended ending (fear as the proper response to the divine encounter) or whether the original ending was lost; the manuscript evidence supports v.8 as the earliest recoverable ending.
16.9:
Note on the longer ending (vv.9–20): Verses 9–20 are absent from the two oldest and most important manuscripts (Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus) and from the oldest Syriac, Coptic, and Armenian witnesses. Many other manuscripts note its absence or mark it as suspect. The vocabulary and style differ from Mark 1–16:8. Most modern text critics regard it as a later addition (perhaps 2nd century) summarizing resurrection appearances from the other Gospels. Its contents are canonical in the church, but its Markan authorship is doubtful.

anastas de prōi / ephainē prōton Mariai tē Magdalēnē — rising early / he appeared first to Mary Magdalene — ephainē (appeared — aorist passive of phainō; also used for resurrection appearances in Matt 28:7). The longer ending begins by identifying the resurrection and the first appearance, filling out what the original ending left unstated.
16.10: ekeinē poretheisa / tois met' autou genomenois / penthounsin kai klaiousin — she having gone / to those who had been with him / mourning and weeping — Mary goes to those who had been with Jesus (tois met' autou genomenois) — a roundabout description of the disciples. Penthounsin kai klaiousin (mourning and weeping — the vocabulary of funeral grief): the disciples are in the posture of bereavement when the resurrection news arrives.
16.11: hoi akousantes hoti zē kai etheathē / ēpistēsan — those hearing that he was alive and had been seen / disbelieved — zē (is alive — present tense) and etheathē (had been seen — aorist passive of theaomai). Ēpistēsan (disbelieved — aorist of apisteō; the negative of pisteuō, to believe). The theme of disbelief at resurrection testimony runs through the longer ending (vv.11, 13, 14) and parallels Luke's Emmaus narrative.
16.12: en hetera morphē / hois ek tōn duo bainousin — in a different form / to two of them as they walked — hetera morphē (different/other form — morphē is the same word as in Phil 2:6–7 for Christ's pre-incarnate and incarnate forms). The reference parallels Luke's Emmaus road account (Luke 24:13–35), though hetera morphē is a unique addition suggesting a changed or transformed appearance.
16.13: oud' ekeinois episteusan — they did not believe them either — the triple disbelief structure (vv.11, 13, 14) before the commission (vv.15–18) follows a pattern: persistent unbelief is overcome only by direct encounter with the risen Lord. The emphasis on the disciples' unbelief at the resurrection is consistent with Mark's characteristic portrayal of their failure to understand throughout the Gospel.
16.14: tois hendeka / ōneidisei tēn apistian autōn kai sklērokardian — to the Eleven / rebuked their unbelief and hardness of heart — hoi hendeka (the Eleven — the formal designation after Judas's defection). Ōneidisen (rebuked, upbraided — a strong verb of reproach). Apistia (unbelief) and sklērokardia (hardness of heart — the word used for divorce in 10:5 and for the Pharisees' hardness in 3:5) are the two sins addressed. Tois theaasamenois auton egēgermenon (those who had seen him raised) — the disciples disbelieved eyewitness testimony.
16.15: poreuthentes eis ton kosmon hapanta / kēruxate to euangelion pasē tē ktisei — going into all the world / proclaim the gospel to all creation — hapanta ton kosmon (all the world — the universal scope). Pasē tē ktisei (to all creation — the most expansive possible framing; ktisis is creation itself, not merely humanity). The commission frames the Gospel's proclamation as cosmically comprehensive, consistent with the longer ending's escalating universalism.
16.16: pisteuō kai baptistheis sōthēsetai / ho de apistēsas katakrthēsetai — the one believing and having been baptized will be saved / the one disbelieving will be condemned — the pairing of pisteuō (faith) and baptistheis (baptism) as conditions of salvation links the two sacramental acts. Notably the negative: ho de apistēsas katakrithēsetai (the one not believing will be condemned) — only unbelief (not unbaptized status) is the basis of condemnation, suggesting faith is the central requirement.
16.17: sēmeia parakolouthēsei tois pisteuousin — signs will follow those who believe — parakolouthēo (to follow alongside, accompany — a word for consistent consequence rather than isolated miracle). The signs listed: casting out demons (daimonia ekbalousin), speaking in new tongues (glōssais lalēsousin kainais), picking up serpents (opheis arousin) — these paralleled the Acts 2 and Acts 28 experiences of the early church.
16.18: opheis arousin / ean ti thansimon piōsin / ou mē autous blapsē / ep' arrōstous cheiras epithēsousin — they will take up snakes / if they drink anything deadly / it will not harm them / they will lay hands on the sick — the list of signs expands on v.17. The handling of snakes echoes Acts 28:3–6 (Paul and the viper at Malta). Ean ti thansimon piōsin (if they drink anything deadly — conditional) — presented as a protective sign, not a command; it became the basis for some later snake-handling sects. Kalōs hexousin (they will be well/recover).
16.19: anelēphthē / ekathisen ek dexiōn tou theou — was taken up / sat at the right hand of God — anelēphthē (was taken up — the same verb as Acts 1:11 for the Ascension; also the verb used for Elijah's translation in 2 Kings 2:9–11 LXX). Ekathisen ek dexiōn tou theou (sat at the right hand of God) — the fulfillment of Psalm 110:1 which Jesus cited in 12:36 (ek dexiōn mou kathō) and his own testimony in 14:62 (ek dexiōn... tēs dynameōs). The longer ending's conclusion thus fulfills Jesus's own predictions.
16.20: ekeinoi de exelthontes ekēruxan pantachou / tou kyriou synergountos kai ton logon bebaiountos — and they going out proclaimed everywhere / the Lord working with them and confirming the word — ekeinoi de (and they — the Eleven) go out and preach everywhere (pantachou). Tou kyriou synergountos (the Lord working together with them — genitive absolute; synergos = co-worker; God as the disciples' fellow-worker). Bebaiountos ton logon (confirming the word — from bebaioō, to make firm, ratify). The longer ending closes with the divine-human synergy of mission: human proclamation, divine confirmation through signs.
